'''Kamisamuraizuka Kofun''' (上侍塚古墳) is a {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳}} located in {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市}}, {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, Japan. Together with {{ill|Shimosamuraizuka Kofun|ja|下侍塚古墳}}, it is part of the National Historic Site "Samuraizuka Kofun." This kofun is notable for being one of the first to undergo a scholarly excavation during the Edo period under the orders of {{ill|Tokugawa Mitsukuni|ja|徳川光圀}}, known as "Mito Kōmon."
== Overview == Kamisamuraizuka Kofun is a large zenpō-kōhō-fun constructed on the right bank of the {{ill|Naka River|ja|那珂川}}. Alongside Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, it represents one of the most significant ancient burial mounds in the Nasu region. Excavations date back to the 5th year of the Genroku era (1692), and further archaeological surveys were conducted starting in 2021.
The mound faces south, with a total length of 114 meters, making it the largest zenpō-kōhō-fun in the Nasu area and the second largest in Tochigi Prefecture after the {{ill|Fujimoto Kannonyama Kofun|ja|藤本観音山古墳}} in {{ill|Ashikaga|ja|足利市}}.<ref name="OhtawaraCity">[https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082767660/ Samuraizuka Kofun] (Ōtawara City website).</ref> The mound is covered with fukiishi (paving stones) and surrounded by a moat approximately 20 meters wide. The burial chamber is believed to have been constructed with clay.<ref name="descriptionboard">Site explanation board.</ref>
Artifacts recovered during the Edo period include bronze mirrors, tubular beads, stone armlets, and iron objects such as armor fragments. These items were reburied in a wooden box following the excavation.<ref name="Asahi20230208">"Rediscovery Efforts for Edo Period Artifacts," ''Asahi Shimbun'' (February 8, 2023).</ref>
== Dimensions == The dimensions of the mound are as follows:<ref name="OhtawaraCity"/><ref name="descriptionboard"/> * '''Mound length:''' 114 meters * '''Posterior section:''' ** Length: 60.5 meters ** Width: 58 meters ** Height: 11.5 meters * '''Anterior section:''' ** Length: 53.5 meters ** Width: 52 meters ** Height: 6.5 meters
The surrounding moat is visible on the north, west, and south sides, while the east side features a natural slope leading to the Naka River.
== Historical Significance == Kamisamuraizuka Kofun, along with Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, is celebrated as the site of Japan's first academically documented archaeological excavation. Under Tokugawa Mitsukuni's orders, the excavation aimed to explore and preserve the cultural heritage of the Nasu region. The discovery of artifacts, followed by their careful documentation and reburial, marks a significant milestone in Japanese archaeology and cultural preservation.<ref name="Asahi20230208"/>
The site was designated a National Historic Site in 1951 as part of the "Samuraizuka Kofun" complex.<ref name="NationalHistoricSite">{{Cite database|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/401/483|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs}}.</ref>
== Related Sites == * '''Shimosamuraizuka Kofun:''' Located nearby, this zenpō-kōhō-fun complements Kamisamuraizuka in historical and archaeological importance. * '''Kamisamuraizuka North Kofun:''' A smaller zenpō-kōhō-fun situated north of Kamisamuraizuka. Little is known about this kofun due to the lack of detailed investigations.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Further Reading == * [https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/115806 "Kamisamuraizuka Kofun Excavation Reports"] (Tochigi Future Creation Foundation, 2021). * [https://kotobank.jp/word/%E4%BE%8D%E5%A1%9A%E5%8F%A4%E5%A2%B3-69803 "Samuraizuka Kofun"] - Kotobank entry.
== External Links == * {{Cite database|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/401/483|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs}} * [https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082767660/ Samuraizuka Kofun] - Ōtawara City website.
{{DEFAULTSORT:Kamisamuraizuka Kofun}} [[Category:kk]][[Category::dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category::Kofun in Tochigi Prefecture]] [[Category:kk]][[Category::dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category::Zenpō-kōhō-fun]] [[Category:kk]][[Category::dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category::Ōtawara City History]] [[Category:kk]][[Category::dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category::National Historic Sites in Tochigi Prefecture]] [[Category:kk]][[Category::dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category::dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category::dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category::Kamisamuraizuka Kofun]][[Category:kk]][[Category::dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category::dd]] [[Category:kk]][[Category::dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category::dd]] [[Category:kkkk]] [[ja:上侍塚古墳]]

'''Kamisamuraizuka Kofun''' (上侍塚古墳) is a {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳}} located in {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市}}, {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, Japan. Together with {{ill|Shimosamuraizuka Kofun|ja|下侍塚古墳}}, it is part of the National Historic Site "Samuraizuka Kofun." This kofun is notable for being one of the first to undergo a scholarly excavation during the Edo period under the orders of {{ill|Tokugawa Mitsukuni|ja|徳川光圀}}, known as "Mito Kōmon."
== Overview == Kamisamuraizuka Kofun is a large zenpō-kōhō-fun constructed on the right bank of the {{ill|Naka River|ja|那珂川}}. Alongside Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, it represents one of the most significant ancient burial mounds in the Nasu region. Excavations date back to the 5th year of the Genroku era (1692), and further archaeological surveys were conducted starting in 2021.
The mound faces south, with a total length of 114 meters, making it the largest zenpō-kōhō-fun in the Nasu area and the second largest in Tochigi Prefecture after the {{ill|Fujimoto Kannonyama Kofun|ja|藤本観音山古墳}} in {{ill|Ashikaga|ja|足利市}}.<ref name="OhtawaraCity">[https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082767660/ Samuraizuka Kofun] (Ōtawara City website).</ref> The mound is covered with fukiishi (paving stones) and surrounded by a moat approximately 20 meters wide. The burial chamber is believed to have been constructed with clay.<ref name="descriptionboard">Site explanation board.</ref>
Artifacts recovered during the Edo period include bronze mirrors, tubular beads, stone armlets, and iron objects such as armor fragments. These items were reburied in a wooden box following the excavation.<ref name="Asahi20230208">"Rediscovery Efforts for Edo Period Artifacts," ''Asahi Shimbun'' (February 8, 2023).</ref>
== Dimensions == The dimensions of the mound are as follows:<ref name="OhtawaraCity"/><ref name="descriptionboard"/> * '''Mound length:''' 114 meters * '''Posterior section:''' ** Length: 60.5 meters ** Width: 58 meters ** Height: 11.5 meters * '''Anterior section:''' ** Length: 53.5 meters ** Width: 52 meters ** Height: 6.5 meters
The surrounding moat is visible on the north, west, and south sides, while the east side features a natural slope leading to the Naka River.
== Historical Significance == Kamisamuraizuka Kofun, along with Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, is celebrated as the site of Japan's first academically documented archaeological excavation. Under Tokugawa Mitsukuni's orders, the excavation aimed to explore and preserve the cultural heritage of the Nasu region. The discovery of artifacts, followed by their careful documentation and reburial, marks a significant milestone in Japanese archaeology and cultural preservation.<ref name="Asahi20230208"/>
The site was designated a National Historic Site in 1951 as part of the "Samuraizuka Kofun" complex.<ref name="NationalHistoricSite">{{Cite database|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/401/483|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs}}.</ref>
== Related Sites == * '''Shimosamuraizuka Kofun:''' Located nearby, this zenpō-kōhō-fun complements Kamisamuraizuka in historical and archaeological importance. * '''Kamisamuraizuka North Kofun:''' A smaller zenpō-kōhō-fun situated north of Kamisamuraizuka. Little is known about this kofun due to the lack of detailed investigations.

== Historical Significance == Kamisamuraizuka Kofun, along with Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, is celebrated as the site of Japan's first academically documented archaeological excavation. Under Tokugawa Mitsukuni's orders, the excavation aimed to explore and preserve the cultural heritage of the Nasu region. The discovery of artifacts, followed by their careful documentation and reburial, marks a significant milestone in Japanese archaeology and cultural preservation.<ref name="Asahi20230208"/>
The site was designated a National Historic Site in 1951 as part of the "Samuraizuka Kofun" complex.<ref name="NationalHistoricSite">{{Cite database|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/401/483|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs}}.</ref>
== Related Sites == * '''Shimosamuraizuka Kofun:''' Located nearby, this zenpō-kōhō-fun complements Kamisamuraizuka in historical and archaeological importance. * '''Kamisamuraizuka North Kofun:''' A smaller zenpō-kōhō-fun situated north of Kamisamuraizuka. Little is known about this kofun due to the lack of detailed investigations.

{{Infobox ancient site |name = Kamisamuraizuka Kofun |image = [[File:Kamisamuraizuka Kofun, funkyu-2.jpg|280px]] |Caption = Mound (left: posterior section, right: anterior section) |Alias = Kurumazuka |Group = |location = {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市}}, {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, Japan |coordinates = {{coord|36|48|21.05|N|140|7|29.45|E|region:JP-09_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |type = {{ill|Zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳}} |Size = Mound length: 114m, height: 11.5m (posterior section) |Burial = Clay chamber |Artifacts = Bronze mirror, tubular beads, stone armlets, iron objects, haji pottery |Subordinate Tombs = |Era = Late 4th century |Buried Person = |Mausoleum = |Historic Designation = Included in the National Historic Site "Samuraizuka Kofun" |Cultural Asset Designation = |Remarks = Second largest zenpō-kōhō-fun in Tochigi Prefecture |Map = Japan Tochigi |Icon = Zenpō-kōhō-fun-S |Label = Kamisamuraizuka Kofun |Label Position = top }}
'''Kamisamuraizuka Kofun''' (上侍塚古墳) is a {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳}} located in {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市}}, {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, Japan. Together with {{ill|Shimosamuraizuka Kofun|ja|下侍塚古墳}}, it is part of the National Historic Site "Samuraizuka Kofun." This kofun is notable for being one of the first to undergo a scholarly excavation during the Edo period under the orders of {{ill|Tokugawa Mitsukuni|ja|徳川光圀}}, known as "Mito Kōmon."
== Overview == Kamisamuraizuka Kofun is a large zenpō-kōhō-fun constructed on the right bank of the {{ill|Naka River|ja|那珂川}}. Alongside Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, it represents one of the most significant ancient burial mounds in the Nasu region. Excavations date back to the 5th year of the Genroku era (1692), and further archaeological surveys were conducted starting in 2021.
The mound faces south, with a total length of 114 meters, making it the largest zenpō-kōhō-fun in the Nasu area and the second largest in Tochigi Prefecture after the {{ill|Fujimoto Kannonyama Kofun|ja|藤本観音山古墳}} in {{ill|Ashikaga|ja|足利市}}.<ref name="OhtawaraCity">[https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082767660/ Samuraizuka Kofun] (Ōtawara City website).</ref> The mound is covered with fukiishi (paving stones) and surrounded by a moat approximately 20 meters wide. The burial chamber is believed to have been constructed with clay.<ref name="descriptionboard">Site explanation board.</ref>
Artifacts recovered during the Edo period include bronze mirrors, tubular beads, stone armlets, and iron objects such as armor fragments. These items were reburied in a wooden box following the excavation.<ref name="Asahi20230208">"Rediscovery Efforts for Edo Period Artifacts," ''Asahi Shimbun'' (February 8, 2023).</ref>
== Dimensions == The dimensions of the mound are as follows:<ref name="OhtawaraCity"/><ref name="descriptionboard"/> * '''Mound length:''' 114 meters * '''Posterior section:''' ** Length: 60.5 meters ** Width: 58 meters ** Height: 11.5 meters * '''Anterior section:''' ** Length: 53.5 meters ** Width: 52 meters ** Height: 6.5 meters
The surrounding moat is visible on the north, west, and south sides, while the east side features a natural slope leading to the Naka River.
== Historical Significance == Kamisamuraizuka Kofun, along with Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, is celebrated as the site of Japan's first academically documented archaeological excavation. Under Tokugawa Mitsukuni's orders, the excavation aimed to explore and preserve the cultural heritage of the Nasu region. The discovery of artifacts, followed by their careful documentation and reburial, marks a significant milestone in Japanese archaeology and cultural preservation.<ref name="Asahi20230208"/>
The site was designated a National Historic Site in 1951 as part of the "Samuraizuka Kofun" complex.<ref name="NationalHistoricSite">{{Cite database|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/401/483|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs}}.</ref>
== Related Sites == * '''Shimosamuraizuka Kofun:''' Located nearby, this zenpō-kōhō-fun complements Kamisamuraizuka in historical and archaeological importance. * '''Kamisamuraizuka North Kofun:''' A smaller zenpō-kōhō-fun situated north of Kamisamuraizuka. Little is known about this kofun due to the lack of detailed investigations.

revision.comment: Adding [[Category:前方後方墳]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:前方後方墳]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Infobox ancient site |name = Kamisamuraizuka Kofun |image = [[File:Kamisamuraizuka Kofun, funkyu-2.jpg|280px]] |Caption = Mound (left: posterior section, right: anterior section) |Alias = Kurumazuka |Group = |location = {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市}}, {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, Japan |coordinates = {{coord|36|48|21.05|N|140|7|29.45|E|region:JP-09_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |type = {{ill|Zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳}} |Size = Mound length: 114m, height: 11.5m (posterior section) |Burial = Clay chamber |Artifacts = Bronze mirror, tubular beads, stone armlets, iron objects, haji pottery |Subordinate Tombs = |Era = Late 4th century |Buried Person = |Mausoleum = |Historic Designation = Included in the National Historic Site "Samuraizuka Kofun" |Cultural Asset Designation = |Remarks = Second largest zenpō-kōhō-fun in Tochigi Prefecture |Map = Japan Tochigi |Icon = Zenpō-kōhō-fun-S |Label = Kamisamuraizuka Kofun |Label Position = top }}
'''Kamisamuraizuka Kofun''' (上侍塚古墳) is a {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳}} located in {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市}}, {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, Japan. Together with {{ill|Shimosamuraizuka Kofun|ja|下侍塚古墳}}, it is part of the National Historic Site "Samuraizuka Kofun." This kofun is notable for being one of the first to undergo a scholarly excavation during the Edo period under the orders of {{ill|Tokugawa Mitsukuni|ja|徳川光圀}}, known as "Mito Kōmon."
== Overview == Kamisamuraizuka Kofun is a large zenpō-kōhō-fun constructed on the right bank of the {{ill|Naka River|ja|那珂川}}. Alongside Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, it represents one of the most significant ancient burial mounds in the Nasu region. Excavations date back to the 5th year of the Genroku era (1692), and further archaeological surveys were conducted starting in 2021.
The mound faces south, with a total length of 114 meters, making it the largest zenpō-kōhō-fun in the Nasu area and the second largest in Tochigi Prefecture after the {{ill|Fujimoto Kannonyama Kofun|ja|藤本観音山古墳}} in {{ill|Ashikaga|ja|足利市}}.<ref name="OhtawaraCity">[https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082767660/ Samuraizuka Kofun] (Ōtawara City website).</ref> The mound is covered with fukiishi (paving stones) and surrounded by a moat approximately 20 meters wide. The burial chamber is believed to have been constructed with clay.<ref name="descriptionboard">Site explanation board.</ref>
Artifacts recovered during the Edo period include bronze mirrors, tubular beads, stone armlets, and iron objects such as armor fragments. These items were reburied in a wooden box following the excavation.<ref name="Asahi20230208">"Rediscovery Efforts for Edo Period Artifacts," ''Asahi Shimbun'' (February 8, 2023).</ref>
== Dimensions == The dimensions of the mound are as follows:<ref name="OhtawaraCity"/><ref name="descriptionboard"/> * '''Mound length:''' 114 meters * '''Posterior section:''' ** Length: 60.5 meters ** Width: 58 meters ** Height: 11.5 meters * '''Anterior section:''' ** Length: 53.5 meters ** Width: 52 meters ** Height: 6.5 meters
The surrounding moat is visible on the north, west, and south sides, while the east side features a natural slope leading to the Naka River.
== Historical Significance == Kamisamuraizuka Kofun, along with Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, is celebrated as the site of Japan's first academically documented archaeological excavation. Under Tokugawa Mitsukuni's orders, the excavation aimed to explore and preserve the cultural heritage of the Nasu region. The discovery of artifacts, followed by their careful documentation and reburial, marks a significant milestone in Japanese archaeology and cultural preservation.<ref name="Asahi20230208"/>
The site was designated a National Historic Site in 1951 as part of the "Samuraizuka Kofun" complex.<ref name="NationalHistoricSite">{{Cite database|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/401/483|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs}}.</ref>
== Related Sites == * '''Shimosamuraizuka Kofun:''' Located nearby, this zenpō-kōhō-fun complements Kamisamuraizuka in historical and archaeological importance. * '''Kamisamuraizuka North Kofun:''' A smaller zenpō-kōhō-fun situated north of Kamisamuraizuka. Little is known about this kofun due to the lack of detailed investigations.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Further Reading == * [https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/115806 "Kamisamuraizuka Kofun Excavation Reports"] (Tochigi Future Creation Foundation, 2021). * [https://kotobank.jp/word/%E4%BE%8D%E5%A1%9A%E5%8F%A4%E5%A2%B3-69803 "Samuraizuka Kofun"] - Kotobank entry.
== External Links == * {{Cite database|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/401/483|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs}} * [https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082767660/ Samuraizuka Kofun] - Ōtawara City website.

revision.text:
{{Infobox ancient site |name = Kamisamuraizuka Kofun |image = [[File:Kamisamuraizuka Kofun, funkyu-2.jpg|280px]] |Caption = Mound (left: posterior section, right: anterior section) |Alias = Kurumazuka |Group = |location = {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市}}, {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, Japan |coordinates = {{coord|36|48|21.05|N|140|7|29.45|E|region:JP-09_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |type = {{ill|Zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳}} |Size = Mound length: 114m, height: 11.5m (posterior section) |Burial = Clay chamber |Artifacts = Bronze mirror, tubular beads, stone armlets, iron objects, haji pottery |Subordinate Tombs = |Era = Late 4th century |Buried Person = |Mausoleum = |Historic Designation = Included in the National Historic Site "Samuraizuka Kofun" |Cultural Asset Designation = |Remarks = Second largest zenpō-kōhō-fun in Tochigi Prefecture |Map = Japan Tochigi |Icon = Zenpō-kōhō-fun-S |Label = Kamisamuraizuka Kofun |Label Position = top }}
'''Kamisamuraizuka Kofun''' (上侍塚古墳) is a {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳}} located in {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市}}, {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, Japan. Together with {{ill|Shimosamuraizuka Kofun|ja|下侍塚古墳}}, it is part of the National Historic Site "Samuraizuka Kofun." This kofun is notable for being one of the first to undergo a scholarly excavation during the Edo period under the orders of {{ill|Tokugawa Mitsukuni|ja|徳川光圀}}, known as "Mito Kōmon."
== Overview == Kamisamuraizuka Kofun is a large zenpō-kōhō-fun constructed on the right bank of the {{ill|Naka River|ja|那珂川}}. Alongside Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, it represents one of the most significant ancient burial mounds in the Nasu region. Excavations date back to the 5th year of the Genroku era (1692), and further archaeological surveys were conducted starting in 2021.
The mound faces south, with a total length of 114 meters, making it the largest zenpō-kōhō-fun in the Nasu area and the second largest in Tochigi Prefecture after the {{ill|Fujimoto Kannonyama Kofun|ja|藤本観音山古墳}} in {{ill|Ashikaga|ja|足利市}}.<ref name="OhtawaraCity">[https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082767660/ Samuraizuka Kofun] (Ōtawara City website).</ref> The mound is covered with fukiishi (paving stones) and surrounded by a moat approximately 20 meters wide. The burial chamber is believed to have been constructed with clay.<ref name="descriptionboard">Site explanation board.</ref>
Artifacts recovered during the Edo period include bronze mirrors, tubular beads, stone armlets, and iron objects such as armor fragments. These items were reburied in a wooden box following the excavation.<ref name="Asahi20230208">"Rediscovery Efforts for Edo Period Artifacts," ''Asahi Shimbun'' (February 8, 2023).</ref>
== Dimensions == The dimensions of the mound are as follows:<ref name="OhtawaraCity"/><ref name="descriptionboard"/> * '''Mound length:''' 114 meters * '''Posterior section:''' ** Length: 60.5 meters ** Width: 58 meters ** Height: 11.5 meters * '''Anterior section:''' ** Length: 53.5 meters ** Width: 52 meters ** Height: 6.5 meters
The surrounding moat is visible on the north, west, and south sides, while the east side features a natural slope leading to the Naka River.
== Historical Significance == Kamisamuraizuka Kofun, along with Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, is celebrated as the site of Japan's first academically documented archaeological excavation. Under Tokugawa Mitsukuni's orders, the excavation aimed to explore and preserve the cultural heritage of the Nasu region. The discovery of artifacts, followed by their careful documentation and reburial, marks a significant milestone in Japanese archaeology and cultural preservation.<ref name="Asahi20230208"/>
The site was designated a National Historic Site in 1951 as part of the "Samuraizuka Kofun" complex.<ref name="NationalHistoricSite">{{Cite database|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/401/483|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs}}.</ref>
== Related Sites == * '''Shimosamuraizuka Kofun:''' Located nearby, this zenpō-kōhō-fun complements Kamisamuraizuka in historical and archaeological importance. * '''Kamisamuraizuka North Kofun:''' A smaller zenpō-kōhō-fun situated north of Kamisamuraizuka. Little is known about this kofun due to the lack of detailed investigations.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Further Reading == * [https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/115806 "Kamisamuraizuka Kofun Excavation Reports"] (Tochigi Future Creation Foundation, 2021). * [https://kotobank.jp/word/%E4%BE%8D%E5%A1%9A%E5%8F%A4%E5%A2%B3-69803 "Samuraizuka Kofun"] - Kotobank entry.
== External Links == * {{Cite database|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/401/483|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs}} * [https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082767660/ Samuraizuka Kofun] - Ōtawara City website.

The burial chamber is believed to have been constructed with clay.<ref name="descriptionboard">Site explanation board.</ref>
Artifacts recovered during the Edo period include bronze mirrors, tubular beads, stone armlets, and iron objects such as armor fragments. These items were reburied in a wooden box following the excavation.<ref name="Asahi20230208">"Rediscovery Efforts for Edo Period Artifacts," ''Asahi Shimbun'' (February 8, 2023).</ref>
== Dimensions == The dimensions of the mound are as follows:<ref name="OhtawaraCity"/><ref name="descriptionboard"/> * '''Mound length:''' 114 meters * '''Posterior section:''' ** Length: 60.5 meters ** Width: 58 meters ** Height: 11.5 meters * '''Anterior section:''' ** Length: 53.5 meters ** Width: 52 meters ** Height: 6.5 meters
The surrounding moat is visible on the north, west, and south sides, while the east side features a natural slope leading to the Naka River.
== Historical Significance == Kamisamuraizuka Kofun, along with Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, is celebrated as the site of Japan's first academically documented archaeological excavation. Under Tokugawa Mitsukuni's orders, the excavation aimed to explore and preserve the cultural heritage of the Nasu region. The discovery of artifacts, followed by their careful documentation and reburial, marks a significant milestone in Japanese archaeology and cultural preservation.<ref name="Asahi20230208"/>
The site was designated a National Historic Site in 1951 as part of the "Samuraizuka Kofun" complex.<ref name="NationalHistoricSite">{{Cite database|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/401/483|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs}}.</ref>
== Related Sites == * '''Shimosamuraizuka Kofun:''' Located nearby, this zenpō-kōhō-fun complements Kamisamuraizuka in historical and archaeological importance. * '''Kamisamuraizuka North Kofun:''' A smaller zenpō-kōhō-fun situated north of Kamisamuraizuka. Little is known about this kofun due to the lack of detailed investigations.

{{Infobox ancient site |name = Kamisamuraizuka Kofun |image = [[File:Kamisamuraizuka Kofun, funkyu-2.jpg|280px]] |Caption = Mound (left: posterior section, right: anterior section) |Alias = Kurumazuka |Group = |location = {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市}}, {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, Japan |coordinates = {{coord|36|48|21.05|N|140|7|29.45|E|region:JP-09_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |type = {{ill|Zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} |Size = Mound length: 114m, height: 11.5m (posterior section) |Burial = Clay chamber |Artifacts = Bronze mirror, tubular beads, stone armlets, iron objects, haji pottery |Subordinate Tombs = |Era = Late 4th century |Buried Person = |Mausoleum = |Historic Designation = Included in the National Historic Site "Samuraizuka Kofun" |Cultural Asset Designation = |Remarks = Second largest zenpō-kōhō-fun in Tochigi Prefecture |Map = Japan Tochigi |Icon = Zenpō-kōhō-fun-S |Label = Kamisamuraizuka Kofun |Label Position = top }}
'''Kamisamuraizuka Kofun''' (上侍塚古墳) is a {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} located in {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市}}, {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, Japan. Together with {{ill|Shimosamuraizuka Kofun|ja|下侍塚古墳}}, it is part of the National Historic Site "Samuraizuka Kofun." This kofun is notable for being one of the first to undergo a scholarly excavation during the Edo period under the orders of {{ill|Tokugawa Mitsukuni|ja|徳川光圀}}, known as "Mito Kōmon."
== Overview == Kamisamuraizuka Kofun is a large zenpō-kōhō-fun constructed on the right bank of the {{ill|Naka River|ja|那珂川}}. Alongside Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, it represents one of the most significant ancient burial mounds in the Nasu region. Excavations date back to the 5th year of the Genroku era (1692), and further archaeological surveys were conducted starting in 2021.
The mound faces south, with a total length of 114 meters, making it the largest zenpō-kōhō-fun in the Nasu area and the second largest in Tochigi Prefecture after the {{ill|Fujimoto Kannonyama Kofun|ja|藤本観音山古墳}} in {{ill|Ashikaga|ja|足利市}}.<ref name="OhtawaraCity">[https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082767660/ Samuraizuka Kofun] (Ōtawara City website).</ref> The mound is covered with fukiishi (paving stones) and surrounded by a moat approximately 20 meters wide. The burial chamber is believed to have been constructed with clay.<ref name="descriptionboard">Site explanation board.</ref>
Artifacts recovered during the Edo period include bronze mirrors, tubular beads, stone armlets, and iron objects such as armor fragments. These items were reburied in a wooden box following the excavation.<ref name="Asahi20230208">"Rediscovery Efforts for Edo Period Artifacts," ''Asahi Shimbun'' (February 8, 2023).</ref>
== Dimensions == The dimensions of the mound are as follows:<ref name="OhtawaraCity"/><ref name="descriptionboard"/> * '''Mound length:''' 114 meters * '''Posterior section:''' ** Length: 60.5 meters ** Width: 58 meters ** Height: 11.5 meters * '''Anterior section:''' ** Length: 53.5 meters ** Width: 52 meters ** Height: 6.5 meters
The surrounding moat is visible on the north, west, and south sides, while the east side features a natural slope leading to the Naka River.
== Historical Significance == Kamisamuraizuka Kofun, along with Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, is celebrated as the site of Japan's first academically documented archaeological excavation. Under Tokugawa Mitsukuni's orders, the excavation aimed to explore and preserve the cultural heritage of the Nasu region. The discovery of artifacts, followed by their careful documentation and reburial, marks a significant milestone in Japanese archaeology and cultural preservation.<ref name="Asahi20230208"/>
The site was designated a National Historic Site in 1951 as part of the "Samuraizuka Kofun" complex.<ref name="NationalHistoricSite">{{Cite database|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/401/483|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs}}.</ref>
== Related Sites == * '''Shimosamuraizuka Kofun:''' Located nearby, this zenpō-kōhō-fun complements Kamisamuraizuka in historical and archaeological importance. * '''Kamisamuraizuka North Kofun:''' A smaller zenpō-kōhō-fun situated north of Kamisamuraizuka. Little is known about this kofun due to the lack of detailed investigations.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Further Reading == * [https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/115806 "Kamisamuraizuka Kofun Excavation Reports"] (Tochigi Future Creation Foundation, 2021). * [https://kotobank.jp/word/%E4%BE%8D%E5%A1%9A%E5%8F%A4%E5%A2%B3-69803 "Samuraizuka Kofun"] - Kotobank entry.

The mound faces south, with a total length of 114 meters, making it the largest zenpō-kōhō-fun in the Nasu area and the second largest in Tochigi Prefecture after the {{ill|Fujimoto Kannonyama Kofun|ja|藤本観音山古墳}} in {{ill|Ashikaga|ja|足利市}}.<ref name="OhtawaraCity">[https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082767660/ Samuraizuka Kofun] (Ōtawara City website).</ref> The mound is covered with fukiishi (paving stones) and surrounded by a moat approximately 20 meters wide. The burial chamber is believed to have been constructed with clay.<ref name="descriptionboard">Site explanation board.</ref>
Artifacts recovered during the Edo period include bronze mirrors, tubular beads, stone armlets, and iron objects such as armor fragments. These items were reburied in a wooden box following the excavation.<ref name="Asahi20230208">"Rediscovery Efforts for Edo Period Artifacts," ''Asahi Shimbun'' (February 8, 2023).</ref>
== Dimensions == The dimensions of the mound are as follows:<ref name="OhtawaraCity"/><ref name="descriptionboard"/> * '''Mound length:''' 114 meters * '''Posterior section:''' ** Length: 60.5 meters ** Width: 58 meters ** Height: 11.5 meters * '''Anterior section:''' ** Length: 53.5 meters ** Width: 52 meters ** Height: 6.5 meters
The surrounding moat is visible on the north, west, and south sides, while the east side features a natural slope leading to the Naka River.
== Historical Significance == Kamisamuraizuka Kofun, along with Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, is celebrated as the site of Japan's first academically documented archaeological excavation. Under Tokugawa Mitsukuni's orders, the excavation aimed to explore and preserve the cultural heritage of the Nasu region. The discovery of artifacts, followed by their careful documentation and reburial, marks a significant milestone in Japanese archaeology and cultural preservation.<ref name="Asahi20230208"/>
The site was designated a National Historic Site in 1951 as part of the "Samuraizuka Kofun" complex.<ref name="NationalHistoricSite">{{Cite database|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/401/483|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs}}.</ref>
== Related Sites == * '''Shimosamuraizuka Kofun:''' Located nearby, this zenpō-kōhō-fun complements Kamisamuraizuka in historical and archaeological importance. * '''Kamisamuraizuka North Kofun:''' A smaller zenpō-kōhō-fun situated north of Kamisamuraizuka. Little is known about this kofun due to the lack of detailed investigations.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Further Reading == * [https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/115806 "Kamisamuraizuka Kofun Excavation Reports"] (Tochigi Future Creation Foundation, 2021). * [https://kotobank.jp/word/%E4%BE%8D%E5%A1%9A%E5%8F%A4%E5%A2%B3-69803 "Samuraizuka Kofun"] - Kotobank entry.

{{Infobox ancient site |name = Kamisamuraizuka Kofun |image = [[File:Kamisamuraizuka Kofun, funkyu-2.jpg|280px]] |Caption = Mound (left: posterior section, right: anterior section) |Alias = Kurumazuka |Group = |location = {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市}}, {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, Japan |coordinates = {{coord|36|48|21.05|N|140|7|29.45|E|region:JP-09_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |type = {{ill|Zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} |Size = Mound length: 114m, height: 11.5m (posterior section) |Burial = Clay chamber |Artifacts = Bronze mirror, tubular beads, stone armlets, iron objects, haji pottery |Subordinate Tombs = |Era = Late 4th century |Buried Person = |Mausoleum = |Historic Designation = Included in the National Historic Site "Samuraizuka Kofun" |Cultural Asset Designation = |Remarks = Second largest zenpō-kōhō-fun in Tochigi Prefecture |Map = Japan Tochigi |Icon = Zenpō-kōhō-fun-S |Label = Kamisamuraizuka Kofun |Label Position = top }}
'''Kamisamuraizuka Kofun''' (上侍塚古墳) is a {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} located in {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市}}, {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, Japan. Together with {{ill|Shimosamuraizuka Kofun|ja|下侍塚古墳}}, it is part of the National Historic Site "Samuraizuka Kofun." This kofun is notable for being one of the first to undergo a scholarly excavation during the Edo period under the orders of {{ill|Tokugawa Mitsukuni|ja|徳川光圀}}, known as "Mito Kōmon."
== Overview == Kamisamuraizuka Kofun is a large zenpō-kōhō-fun constructed on the right bank of the {{ill|Naka River|ja|那珂川}}. Alongside Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, it represents one of the most significant ancient Kofuns in the Nasu region. Excavations date back to the 5th year of the Genroku era (1692), and further archaeological surveys were conducted starting in 2021.
The mound faces south, with a total length of 114 meters, making it the largest zenpō-kōhō-fun in the Nasu area and the second largest in Tochigi Prefecture after the {{ill|Fujimoto Kannonyama Kofun|ja|藤本観音山古墳}} in {{ill|Ashikaga|ja|足利市}}.<ref name="OhtawaraCity">[https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082767660/ Samuraizuka Kofun] (Ōtawara City website).</ref> The mound is covered with fukiishi (paving stones) and surrounded by a moat approximately 20 meters wide. The burial chamber is believed to have been constructed with clay.<ref name="descriptionboard">Site explanation board.</ref>
Artifacts recovered during the Edo period include bronze mirrors, tubular beads, stone armlets, and iron objects such as armor fragments. These items were reburied in a wooden box following the excavation.<ref name="Asahi20230208">"Rediscovery Efforts for Edo Period Artifacts," ''Asahi Shimbun'' (February 8, 2023).</ref>
== Dimensions == The dimensions of the mound are as follows:<ref name="OhtawaraCity"/><ref name="descriptionboard"/> * '''Mound length:''' 114 meters * '''Posterior section:''' ** Length: 60.5 meters ** Width: 58 meters ** Height: 11.5 meters * '''Anterior section:''' ** Length: 53.5 meters ** Width: 52 meters ** Height: 6.5 meters
The surrounding moat is visible on the north, west, and south sides, while the east side features a natural slope leading to the Naka River.
== Historical Significance == Kamisamuraizuka Kofun, along with Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, is celebrated as the site of Japan's first academically documented archaeological excavation. Under Tokugawa Mitsukuni's orders, the excavation aimed to explore and preserve the cultural heritage of the Nasu region. The discovery of artifacts, followed by their careful documentation and reburial, marks a significant milestone in Japanese archaeology and cultural preservation.<ref name="Asahi20230208"/>
The site was designated a National Historic Site in 1951 as part of the "Samuraizuka Kofun" complex.<ref name="NationalHistoricSite">{{Cite database|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/401/483|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs}}.</ref>
== Related Sites == * '''Shimosamuraizuka Kofun:''' Located nearby, this zenpō-kōhō-fun complements Kamisamuraizuka in historical and archaeological importance. * '''Kamisamuraizuka North Kofun:''' A smaller zenpō-kōhō-fun situated north of Kamisamuraizuka. Little is known about this kofun due to the lack of detailed investigations.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Further Reading == * [https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/115806 "Kamisamuraizuka Kofun Excavation Reports"] (Tochigi Future Creation Foundation, 2021). * [https://kotobank.jp/word/%E4%BE%8D%E5%A1%9A%E5%8F%A4%E5%A2%B3-69803 "Samuraizuka Kofun"] - Kotobank entry.
== External Links == * {{Cite database|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/401/483|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs}} * [https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082767660/ Samuraizuka Kofun] - Ōtawara City website.

The burial chamber is believed to have been constructed with clay.<ref name="descriptionboard">Site explanation board.</ref>
Artifacts recovered during the Edo period include bronze mirrors, tubular beads, stone armlets, and iron objects such as armor fragments. These items were reburied in a wooden box following the excavation.<ref name="Asahi20230208">"Rediscovery Efforts for Edo Period Artifacts," ''Asahi Shimbun'' (February 8, 2023).</ref>
== Dimensions == The dimensions of the mound are as follows:<ref name="OhtawaraCity"/><ref name="descriptionboard"/> * '''Mound length:''' 114 meters * '''Posterior section:''' ** Length: 60.5 meters ** Width: 58 meters ** Height: 11.5 meters * '''Anterior section:''' ** Length: 53.5 meters ** Width: 52 meters ** Height: 6.5 meters
The surrounding moat is visible on the north, west, and south sides, while the east side features a natural slope leading to the Naka River.
== Historical Significance == Kamisamuraizuka Kofun, along with Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, is celebrated as the site of Japan's first academically documented archaeological excavation. Under Tokugawa Mitsukuni's orders, the excavation aimed to explore and preserve the cultural heritage of the Nasu region. The discovery of artifacts, followed by their careful documentation and reburial, marks a significant milestone in Japanese archaeology and cultural preservation.<ref name="Asahi20230208"/>
The site was designated a National Historic Site in 1951 as part of the "Samuraizuka Kofun" complex.<ref name="NationalHistoricSite">{{Cite database|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/401/483|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs}}.</ref>
== Related Sites == * '''Shimosamuraizuka Kofun:''' Located nearby, this zenpō-kōhō-fun complements Kamisamuraizuka in historical and archaeological importance. * '''Kamisamuraizuka North Kofun:''' A smaller zenpō-kōhō-fun situated north of Kamisamuraizuka. Little is known about this kofun due to the lack of detailed investigations.

{{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun}} |name = Kamisamuraizuka Kofun |image = [[File:Kamisamuraizuka Kofun, funkyu-2.jpg|280px]] |Caption = Mound (left: posterior section, right: anterior section) |Alias = Kurumazuka |Group = |location = {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市}}, {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, Japan |coordinates = {{coord|36|48|21.05|N|140|7|29.45|E|region:JP-09_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |type = {{ill|Zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} |Size = Mound length: 114m, height: 11.5m (posterior section) |Burial = Clay chamber |Artifacts = Bronze mirror, tubular beads, stone armlets, iron objects, haji pottery |Subordinate Tombs = |Era = Late 4th century |Buried Person = |Mausoleum = |Historic Designation = Included in the National Historic Site "Samuraizuka Kofun" |Cultural Asset Designation = |Remarks = Second largest zenpō-kōhō-fun in Tochigi Prefecture |Map = Japan Tochigi |Icon = Zenpō-kōhō-fun-S |Label = Kamisamuraizuka Kofun |Label Position = top }}
'''Kamisamuraizuka Kofun''' (上侍塚古墳) is a {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} located in {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市}}, {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, Japan. Together with {{ill|Shimosamuraizuka Kofun|ja|下侍塚古墳}}, it is part of the National Historic Site "Samuraizuka Kofun." This kofun is notable for being one of the first to undergo a scholarly excavation during the Edo period under the orders of {{ill|Tokugawa Mitsukuni|ja|徳川光圀}}, known as "Mito Kōmon."
== Overview == Kamisamuraizuka Kofun is a large zenpō-kōhō-fun constructed on the right bank of the {{ill|Naka River|ja|那珂川}}. Alongside Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, it represents one of the most significant ancient Kofuns in the Nasu region. Excavations date back to the 5th year of the Genroku era (1692), and further archaeological surveys were conducted starting in 2021.
The mound faces south, with a total length of 114 meters, making it the largest zenpō-kōhō-fun in the Nasu area and the second largest in Tochigi Prefecture after the {{ill|Fujimoto Kannonyama Kofun|ja|藤本観音山古墳}} in {{ill|Ashikaga|ja|足利市}}.<ref name="OhtawaraCity">[https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082767660/ Samuraizuka Kofun] (Ōtawara City website).</ref> The mound is covered with fukiishi (paving stones) and surrounded by a moat approximately 20 meters wide. The burial chamber is believed to have been constructed with clay.<ref name="descriptionboard">Site explanation board.</ref>
Artifacts recovered during the Edo period include bronze mirrors, tubular beads, stone armlets, and iron objects such as armor fragments. These items were reburied in a wooden box following the excavation.<ref name="Asahi20230208">"Rediscovery Efforts for Edo Period Artifacts," ''Asahi Shimbun'' (February 8, 2023).</ref>
== Dimensions == The dimensions of the mound are as follows:<ref name="OhtawaraCity"/><ref name="descriptionboard"/> * '''Mound length:''' 114 meters * '''Posterior section:''' ** Length: 60.5 meters ** Width: 58 meters ** Height: 11.5 meters * '''Anterior section:''' ** Length: 53.5 meters ** Width: 52 meters ** Height: 6.5 meters
The surrounding moat is visible on the north, west, and south sides, while the east side features a natural slope leading to the Naka River.
== Historical Significance == Kamisamuraizuka Kofun, along with Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, is celebrated as the site of Japan's first academically documented archaeological excavation. Under Tokugawa Mitsukuni's orders, the excavation aimed to explore and preserve the cultural heritage of the Nasu region. The discovery of artifacts, followed by their careful documentation and reburial, marks a significant milestone in Japanese archaeology and cultural preservation.<ref name="Asahi20230208"/>
The site was designated a National Historic Site in 1951 as part of the "Samuraizuka Kofun" complex.<ref name="NationalHistoricSite">{{Cite database|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/401/483|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs}}.</ref>
== Related Sites == * '''Shimosamuraizuka Kofun:''' Located nearby, this zenpō-kōhō-fun complements Kamisamuraizuka in historical and archaeological importance. * '''Kamisamuraizuka North Kofun:''' A smaller zenpō-kōhō-fun situated north of Kamisamuraizuka. Little is known about this kofun due to the lack of detailed investigations.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Further Reading == * [https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/115806 "Kamisamuraizuka Kofun Excavation Reports"] (Tochigi Future Creation Foundation, 2021). * [https://kotobank.jp/word/%E4%BE%8D%E5%A1%9A%E5%8F%A4%E5%A2%B3-69803 "Samuraizuka Kofun"] - Kotobank entry.
== External Links == * {{Cite database|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/401/483|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs}} * [https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082767660/ Samuraizuka Kofun] - Ōtawara City website.

== Related Sites == * '''Shimosamuraizuka Kofun:''' Located nearby, this zenpō-kōhō-fun complements Kamisamuraizuka in historical and archaeological importance. * '''Kamisamuraizuka North Kofun:''' A smaller zenpō-kōhō-fun situated north of Kamisamuraizuka. Little is known about this kofun due to the lack of detailed investigations.

This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun}} |name = Kamisamuraizuka Kofun |image = [[File:Kamisamuraizuka Kofun, funkyu-2.jpg|280px]] |Caption = Mound (left: posterior section, right: anterior section) |Alias = Kurumazuka |Group = |location = {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市}}, {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, Japan |coordinates = {{coord|36|48|21.05|N|140|7|29.45|E|region:JP-09_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |type = {{ill|Zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} |Size = Mound length: 114m, height: 11.5m (posterior section) |Burial = Clay chamber |Artifacts = Bronze mirror, tubular beads, stone armlets, iron objects, haji pottery |Subordinate Tombs = |Era = Late 4th century |Buried Person = |Mausoleum = |Historic Designation = Included in the National Historic Site "Samuraizuka Kofun" |Cultural Asset Designation = |Remarks = Second largest zenpō-kōhō-fun in Tochigi Prefecture |Map = Japan Tochigi |Icon = Zenpō-kōhō-fun-S |Label = Kamisamuraizuka Kofun |Label Position = top }}
'''Kamisamuraizuka Kofun''' (上侍塚古墳) is a {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} located in {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市}}, {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, Japan. Together with {{ill|Shimosamuraizuka Kofun|ja|下侍塚古墳}}, it is part of the National Historic Site "Samuraizuka Kofun." This kofun is notable for being one of the first to undergo a scholarly excavation during the Edo period under the orders of {{ill|Tokugawa Mitsukuni|ja|徳川光圀}}, known as "Mito Kōmon."
== Overview == Kamisamuraizuka Kofun is a large zenpō-kōhō-fun constructed on the right bank of the {{ill|Naka River|ja|那珂川}}. Alongside Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, it represents one of the most significant ancient Kofuns in the Nasu region. Excavations date back to the 5th year of the Genroku era (1692), and further archaeological surveys were conducted starting in 2021.
The mound faces south, with a total length of 114 meters, making it the largest zenpō-kōhō-fun in the Nasu area and the second largest in Tochigi Prefecture after the {{ill|Fujimoto Kannonyama Kofun|ja|藤本観音山古墳}} in {{ill|Ashikaga|ja|足利市}}.<ref name="OhtawaraCity">[https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082767660/ Samuraizuka Kofun] (Ōtawara City website).</ref> The mound is covered with fukiishi (paving stones) and surrounded by a moat approximately 20 meters wide. The burial chamber is believed to have been constructed with clay.<ref name="descriptionboard">Site explanation board.</ref>
Artifacts recovered during the Edo period include bronze mirrors, tubular beads, stone armlets, and iron objects such as armor fragments. These items were reburied in a wooden box following the excavation.<ref name="Asahi20230208">"Rediscovery Efforts for Edo Period Artifacts," ''Asahi Shimbun'' (February 8, 2023).</ref>
== Dimensions == The dimensions of the mound are as follows:<ref name="OhtawaraCity"/><ref name="descriptionboard"/> * '''Mound length:''' 114 meters * '''Posterior section:''' ** Length: 60.5 meters ** Width: 58 meters ** Height: 11.5 meters * '''Anterior section:''' ** Length: 53.5 meters ** Width: 52 meters ** Height: 6.5 meters
The surrounding moat is visible on the north, west, and south sides, while the east side features a natural slope leading to the Naka River.
== Historical Significance == Kamisamuraizuka Kofun, along with Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, is celebrated as the site of Japan's first academically documented archaeological excavation. Under Tokugawa Mitsukuni's orders, the excavation aimed to explore and preserve the cultural heritage of the Nasu region. The discovery of artifacts, followed by their careful documentation and reburial, marks a significant milestone in Japanese archaeology and cultural preservation.<ref name="Asahi20230208"/>
The site was designated a National Historic Site in 1951 as part of the "Samuraizuka Kofun" complex.<ref name="NationalHistoricSite">{{Cite database|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/401/483|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs}}.</ref>
== Related Sites == * '''Shimosamuraizuka Kofun:''' Located nearby, this zenpō-kōhō-fun complements Kamisamuraizuka in historical and archaeological importance. * '''Kamisamuraizuka North Kofun:''' A smaller zenpō-kōhō-fun situated north of Kamisamuraizuka. Little is known about this kofun due to the lack of detailed investigations.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Further Reading == * [https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/115806 "Kamisamuraizuka Kofun Excavation Reports"] (Tochigi Future Creation Foundation, 2021). * [https://kotobank.jp/word/%E4%BE%8D%E5%A1%9A%E5%8F%A4%E5%A2%B3-69803 "Samuraizuka Kofun"] - Kotobank entry.
== External Links == * {{Cite database|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/401/483|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs}} * [https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082767660/ Samuraizuka Kofun] - Ōtawara City website.

This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun}} |name = Kamisamuraizuka Kofun |image = [[File:Kamisamuraizuka Kofun, funkyu-2.jpg|280px]] |Caption = Mound (left: posterior section, right: anterior section) |Alias = Kurumazuka |Group = |location = {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市}}, {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, Japan |coordinates = {{coord|36|48|21.05|N|140|7|29.45|E|region:JP-09_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |type = {{ill|Zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} |Size = Mound length: 114m, height: 11.5m (posterior section) |Burial = Clay chamber |burial goods = Bronze mirror, tubular beads, stone armlets, iron objects, haji pottery |Subordinate Tombs = |Era = Late 4th century |Buried Person = |Mausoleum = |Historic Designation = Included in the National Historic Site "Samuraizuka Kofun" |Cultural Asset Designation = |Remarks = Second largest zenpō-kōhō-fun in Tochigi Prefecture |Map = Japan Tochigi |Icon = Zenpō-kōhō-fun-S |Label = Kamisamuraizuka Kofun |Label Position = top }}
'''Kamisamuraizuka Kofun''' (上侍塚古墳) is a {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} located in {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市}}, {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, Japan. Together with {{ill|Shimosamuraizuka Kofun|ja|下侍塚古墳}}, it is part of the National Historic Site "Samuraizuka Kofun." This kofun is notable for being one of the first to undergo a scholarly excavation during the Edo period under the orders of {{ill|Tokugawa Mitsukuni|ja|徳川光圀}}, known as "Mito Kōmon."
== Overview == Kamisamuraizuka Kofun is a large zenpō-kōhō-fun constructed on the right bank of the {{ill|Naka River|ja|那珂川}}. Alongside Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, it represents one of the most significant ancient Kofuns in the Nasu region. Excavations date back to the 5th year of the Genroku era (1692), and further archaeological surveys were conducted starting in 2021.
The mound faces south, with a total length of 114 meters, making it the largest zenpō-kōhō-fun in the Nasu area and the second largest in Tochigi Prefecture after the {{ill|Fujimoto Kannonyama Kofun|ja|藤本観音山古墳}} in {{ill|Ashikaga|ja|足利市}}.<ref name="OhtawaraCity">[https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082767660/ Samuraizuka Kofun] (Ōtawara City website).</ref> The mound is covered with fukiishi (paving stones) and surrounded by a moat approximately 20 meters wide. The burial chamber is believed to have been constructed with clay.<ref name="descriptionboard">Site explanation board.</ref>
Artifacts recovered during the Edo period include bronze mirrors, tubular beads, stone armlets, and iron objects such as armor fragments. These items were reburied in a wooden box following the excavation.<ref name="Asahi20230208">"Rediscovery Efforts for Edo Period Artifacts," ''Asahi Shimbun'' (February 8, 2023).</ref>
== Dimensions == The dimensions of the mound are as follows:<ref name="OhtawaraCity"/><ref name="descriptionboard"/> * '''Mound length:''' 114 meters * '''Posterior section:''' ** Length: 60.5 meters ** Width: 58 meters ** Height: 11.5 meters * '''Anterior section:''' ** Length: 53.5 meters ** Width: 52 meters ** Height: 6.5 meters
The surrounding moat is visible on the north, west, and south sides, while the east side features a natural slope leading to the Naka River.
== Historical Significance == Kamisamuraizuka Kofun, along with Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, is celebrated as the site of Japan's first academically documented archaeological excavation. Under Tokugawa Mitsukuni's orders, the excavation aimed to explore and preserve the cultural heritage of the Nasu region. The discovery of artifacts, followed by their careful documentation and reburial, marks a significant milestone in Japanese archaeology and cultural preservation.<ref name="Asahi20230208"/>
The site was designated a National Historic Site in 1951 as part of the "Samuraizuka Kofun" complex.<ref name="NationalHistoricSite">{{Cite database|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/401/483|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs}}.</ref>
== Related Sites == * '''Shimosamuraizuka Kofun:''' Located nearby, this zenpō-kōhō-fun complements Kamisamuraizuka in historical and archaeological importance. * '''Kamisamuraizuka North Kofun:''' A smaller zenpō-kōhō-fun situated north of Kamisamuraizuka. Little is known about this kofun due to the lack of detailed investigations.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Further Reading == * [https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/115806 "Kamisamuraizuka Kofun Excavation Reports"] (Tochigi Future Creation Foundation, 2021). * [https://kotobank.jp/word/%E4%BE%8D%E5%A1%9A%E5%8F%A4%E5%A2%B3-69803 "Samuraizuka Kofun"] - Kotobank entry.
== External Links == * {{Cite database|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/401/483|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs}} * [https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082767660/ Samuraizuka Kofun] - Ōtawara City website.

This kofun is notable for being one of the first to undergo a scholarly excavation during the Edo period under the orders of {{ill|Tokugawa Mitsukuni|ja|徳川光圀}}, known as "Mito Kōmon."
== Overview == Kamisamuraizuka Kofun is a large zenpō-kōhō-fun constructed on the right bank of the {{ill|Naka River|ja|那珂川}}. Alongside Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, it represents one of the most significant ancient Kofuns in the Nasu region. Excavations date back to the 5th year of the Genroku era (1692), and further archaeological surveys were conducted starting in 2021.
The mound faces south, with a total length of 114 meters, making it the largest zenpō-kōhō-fun in the Nasu area and the second largest in Tochigi Prefecture after the {{ill|Fujimoto Kannonyama Kofun|ja|藤本観音山古墳}} in {{ill|Ashikaga|ja|足利市}}.<ref name="OhtawaraCity">[https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082767660/ Samuraizuka Kofun] (Ōtawara City website).</ref> The mound is covered with fukiishi (paving stones) and surrounded by a moat approximately 20 meters wide. The burial chamber is believed to have been constructed with clay.<ref name="descriptionboard">Site explanation board.</ref>
Artifacts recovered during the Edo period include bronze mirrors, tubular beads, stone armlets, and iron objects such as armor fragments. These items were reburied in a wooden box following the excavation.<ref name="Asahi20230208">"Rediscovery Efforts for Edo Period Artifacts," ''Asahi Shimbun'' (February 8, 2023).</ref>
== Dimensions == The dimensions of the mound are as follows:<ref name="OhtawaraCity"/><ref name="descriptionboard"/> * '''Mound length:''' 114 meters * '''Posterior section:''' ** Length: 60.5 meters ** Width: 58 meters ** Height: 11.5 meters * '''Anterior section:''' ** Length: 53.5 meters ** Width: 52 meters ** Height: 6.5 meters
The surrounding moat is visible on the north, west, and south sides, while the east side features a natural slope leading to the Naka River.
== Historical Significance == Kamisamuraizuka Kofun, along with Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, is celebrated as the site of Japan's first academically documented archaeological excavation. Under Tokugawa Mitsukuni's orders, the excavation aimed to explore and preserve the cultural heritage of the Nasu region. The discovery of artifacts, followed by their careful documentation and reburial, marks a significant milestone in Japanese archaeology and cultural preservation.<ref name="Asahi20230208"/>
The site was designated a National Historic Site in 1951 as part of the "Samuraizuka Kofun" complex.<ref name="NationalHistoricSite">{{Cite database|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/401/483|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs}}.</ref>
== Related Sites == * '''Shimosamuraizuka Kofun:''' Located nearby, this zenpō-kōhō-fun complements Kamisamuraizuka in historical and archaeological importance. * '''Kamisamuraizuka North Kofun:''' A smaller zenpō-kōhō-fun situated north of Kamisamuraizuka. Little is known about this kofun due to the lack of detailed investigations.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Further Reading == * [https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/115806 "Kamisamuraizuka Kofun Excavation Reports"] (Tochigi Future Creation Foundation, 2021). * [https://kotobank.jp/word/%E4%BE%8D%E5%A1%9A%E5%8F%A4%E5%A2%B3-69803 "Samuraizuka Kofun"] - Kotobank entry.
== External Links == * {{Cite database|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/401/483|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs}} * [https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082767660/ Samuraizuka Kofun] - Ōtawara City website.

== Historical Significance == Kamisamuraizuka Kofun, along with Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, is celebrated as the site of Japan's first academically documented archaeological excavation. Under Tokugawa Mitsukuni's orders, the excavation aimed to explore and preserve the cultural heritage of the Nasu region. The discovery of artifacts, followed by their careful documentation and reburial, marks a significant milestone in Japanese archaeology and cultural preservation.<ref name="Asahi20230208"/>
The site was designated a National Historic Site in 1951 as part of the "Samuraizuka Kofun" complex.<ref name="NationalHistoricSite">{{Cite database|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/401/483|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs}}.</ref>
== Related Sites == * '''Shimosamuraizuka Kofun:''' Located nearby, this zenpō-kōhō-fun complements Kamisamuraizuka in historical and archaeological importance. * '''Kamisamuraizuka North Kofun:''' A smaller zenpō-kōhō-fun situated north of Kamisamuraizuka. Little is known about this kofun due to the lack of detailed investigations.

== Further Reading == * [https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/115806 "Kamisamuraizuka Kofun Excavation Reports"] (Tochigi Future Creation Foundation, 2021). * [https://kotobank.jp/word/%E4%BE%8D%E5%A1%9A%E5%8F%A4%E5%A2%B3-69803 "Samuraizuka Kofun"] - Kotobank entry.

'''Kamisamuraizuka Kofun''' (上侍塚古墳) is a {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} located in {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市}}, {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, Japan. Together with {{ill|Shimosamuraizuka Kofun|ja|下侍塚古墳}}, it is part of the National Historic Site "Samuraizuka Kofun." This kofun is notable for being one of the first to undergo a scholarly excavation during the Edo period under the orders of {{ill|Tokugawa Mitsukuni|ja|徳川光圀}}, known as "Mito Kōmon."
== Overview == Kamisamuraizuka Kofun is a large zenpō-kōhō-fun constructed on the right bank of the {{ill|Naka River|ja|那珂川}}. Alongside Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, it represents one of the most significant ancient Kofuns in the Nasu region. Excavations date back to the 5th year of the Genroku era (1692), and further archaeological surveys were conducted starting in 2021.
The mound faces south, with a total length of 114 meters, making it the largest zenpō-kōhō-fun in the Nasu area and the second largest in Tochigi Prefecture after the {{ill|Fujimoto Kannonyama Kofun|ja|藤本観音山古墳}} in {{ill|Ashikaga|ja|足利市}}.<ref name="OhtawaraCity">[https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082767660/ Samuraizuka Kofun] (Ōtawara City website).</ref> The mound is covered with fukiishi (paving stones) and surrounded by a moat approximately 20 meters wide. The burial chamber is believed to have been constructed with clay.<ref name="descriptionboard">Site explanation board.</ref>
Artifacts recovered during the Edo period include bronze mirrors, tubular beads, stone armlets, and iron objects such as armor fragments. These items were reburied in a wooden box following the excavation.<ref name="Asahi20230208">"Rediscovery Efforts for Edo Period Artifacts," ''Asahi Shimbun'' (February 8, 2023).</ref>
== area == The dimensions of the mound are as follows:<ref name="OhtawaraCity"/><ref name="descriptionboard"/> * '''Mound length:''' 114 meters * '''Posterior section:''' ** Length: 60.5 meters ** Width: 58 meters ** Height: 11.5 meters * '''Anterior section:''' ** Length: 53.5 meters ** Width: 52 meters ** Height: 6.5 meters
The surrounding moat is visible on the north, west, and south sides, while the east side features a natural slope leading to the Naka River.
== Historical Significance == Kamisamuraizuka Kofun, along with Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, is celebrated as the site of Japan's first academically documented archaeological excavation. Under Tokugawa Mitsukuni's orders, the excavation aimed to explore and preserve the cultural heritage of the Nasu region. The discovery of artifacts, followed by their careful documentation and reburial, marks a significant milestone in Japanese archaeology and cultural preservation.<ref name="Asahi20230208"/>
The site was designated a National Historic Site in 1951 as part of the "Samuraizuka Kofun" complex.<ref name="NationalHistoricSite">{{Cite database|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/401/483|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs}}.</ref>
== Related Sites == * '''Shimosamuraizuka Kofun:''' Located nearby, this zenpō-kōhō-fun complements Kamisamuraizuka in historical and archaeological importance. * '''Kamisamuraizuka North Kofun:''' A smaller zenpō-kōhō-fun situated north of Kamisamuraizuka. Little is known about this kofun due to the lack of detailed investigations.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Further Reading == * [https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/115806 "Kamisamuraizuka Kofun Excavation Reports"] (Tochigi Future Creation Foundation, 2021). * [https://kotobank.jp/word/%E4%BE%8D%E5%A1%9A%E5%8F%A4%E5%A2%B3-69803 "Samuraizuka Kofun"] - Kotobank entry.
== External Links == * {{Cite database|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/401/483|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs}} * [https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082767660/ Samuraizuka Kofun] - Ōtawara City website.

Please do not use any bots that just remove the unknown parameters without looking at them. This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun}} |name = Kamisamuraizuka Kofun |image = [[File:Kamisamuraizuka Kofun, funkyu-2.jpg|280px]] |Caption = Mound (left: posterior section, right: anterior section) |Alias = Kurumazuka |Group = |location = {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市}}, {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, Japan |coordinates = {{coord|36|48|21.05|N|140|7|29.45|E|region:JP-09_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |type = {{ill|Zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} |area = Mound length: 114m, height: 11.5m (posterior section) |Burial = Clay chamber |burial goods = Bronze mirror, tubular beads, stone armlets, iron objects, haji pottery |Subordinate Tombs = |Era = Late 4th century |Buried Person = |Mausoleum = |Historic Designation = Included in the National Historic Site "Samuraizuka Kofun" |Cultural Asset Designation = |Remarks = Second largest zenpō-kōhō-fun in Tochigi Prefecture |map = Japan Tochigi |Icon = Zenpō-kōhō-fun-S |map_dot_label = Kamisamuraizuka Kofun |map_dot_label = top }}
'''Kamisamuraizuka Kofun''' (上侍塚古墳) is a {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} located in {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市}}, {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, Japan. Together with {{ill|Shimosamuraizuka Kofun|ja|下侍塚古墳}}, it is part of the National Historic Site "Samuraizuka Kofun." This kofun is notable for being one of the first to undergo a scholarly excavation during the Edo period under the orders of {{ill|Tokugawa Mitsukuni|ja|徳川光圀}}, known as "Mito Kōmon."
== Overview == Kamisamuraizuka Kofun is a large zenpō-kōhō-fun constructed on the right bank of the {{ill|Naka River|ja|那珂川}}. Alongside Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, it represents one of the most significant ancient Kofuns in the Nasu region. Excavations date back to the 5th year of the Genroku era (1692), and further archaeological surveys were conducted starting in 2021.
The mound faces south, with a total length of 114 meters, making it the largest zenpō-kōhō-fun in the Nasu area and the second largest in Tochigi Prefecture after the {{ill|Fujimoto Kannonyama Kofun|ja|藤本観音山古墳}} in {{ill|Ashikaga|ja|足利市}}.<ref name="OhtawaraCity">[https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082767660/ Samuraizuka Kofun] (Ōtawara City website).</ref> The mound is covered with fukiishi (paving stones) and surrounded by a moat approximately 20 meters wide. The burial chamber is believed to have been constructed with clay.<ref name="descriptionboard">Site explanation board.</ref>
Artifacts recovered during the Edo period include bronze mirrors, tubular beads, stone armlets, and iron objects such as armor fragments. These items were reburied in a wooden box following the excavation.<ref name="Asahi20230208">"Rediscovery Efforts for Edo Period Artifacts," ''Asahi Shimbun'' (February 8, 2023).</ref>
== area == The dimensions of the mound are as follows:<ref name="OhtawaraCity"/><ref name="descriptionboard"/> * '''Mound length:''' 114 meters * '''Posterior section:''' ** Length: 60.5 meters ** Width: 58 meters ** Height: 11.5 meters * '''Anterior section:''' ** Length: 53.5 meters ** Width: 52 meters ** Height: 6.5 meters
The surrounding moat is visible on the north, west, and south sides, while the east side features a natural slope leading to the Naka River.
== Historical Significance == Kamisamuraizuka Kofun, along with Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, is celebrated as the site of Japan's first academically documented archaeological excavation. Under Tokugawa Mitsukuni's orders, the excavation aimed to explore and preserve the cultural heritage of the Nasu region. The discovery of artifacts, followed by their careful documentation and reburial, marks a significant milestone in Japanese archaeology and cultural preservation.<ref name="Asahi20230208"/>
The site was designated a National Historic Site in 1951 as part of the "Samuraizuka Kofun" complex.<ref name="NationalHistoricSite">{{Cite database|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/401/483|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs}}.</ref>
== Related Sites == * '''Shimosamuraizuka Kofun:''' Located nearby, this zenpō-kōhō-fun complements Kamisamuraizuka in historical and archaeological importance. * '''Kamisamuraizuka North Kofun:''' A smaller zenpō-kōhō-fun situated north of Kamisamuraizuka. Little is known about this kofun due to the lack of detailed investigations.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Further Reading == * [https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/115806 "Kamisamuraizuka Kofun Excavation Reports"] (Tochigi Future Creation Foundation, 2021). * [https://kotobank.jp/word/%E4%BE%8D%E5%A1%9A%E5%8F%A4%E5%A2%B3-69803 "Samuraizuka Kofun"] - Kotobank entry.
== External Links == * {{Cite database|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/401/483|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs}} * [https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082767660/ Samuraizuka Kofun] - Ōtawara City website.

'''Kamisamuraizuka Kofun''' (上侍塚古墳) is a {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} located in {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市}}, {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, Japan. Together with {{ill|Shimosamuraizuka Kofun|ja|下侍塚古墳}}, it is part of the National Historic Site "Samuraizuka Kofun." This kofun is notable for being one of the first to undergo a scholarly excavation during the Edo period under the orders of {{ill|Tokugawa Mitsukuni|ja|徳川光圀}}, known as "Mito Kōmon."
== Overview == Kamisamuraizuka Kofun is a large zenpō-kōhō-fun constructed on the right bank of the {{ill|Naka River|ja|那珂川}}. Alongside Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, it represents one of the most significant ancient Kofuns in the Nasu region. Excavations date back to the 5th year of the Genroku era (1692), and further archaeological surveys were conducted starting in 2021.
The mound faces south, with a total length of 114 meters, making it the largest zenpō-kōhō-fun in the Nasu area and the second largest in Tochigi Prefecture after the {{ill|Fujimoto Kannonyama Kofun|ja|藤本観音山古墳}} in {{ill|Ashikaga|ja|足利市}}.<ref name="OhtawaraCity">[https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082767660/ Samuraizuka Kofun] (Ōtawara City website).</ref> The mound is covered with fukiishi (paving stones) and surrounded by a moat approximately 20 meters wide. The burial chamber is believed to have been constructed with clay.<ref name="descriptionboard">Site explanation board.</ref>
Artifacts recovered during the Edo period include bronze mirrors, tubular beads, stone armlets, and iron objects such as armor fragments. These items were reburied in a wooden box following the excavation.<ref name="Asahi20230208">"Rediscovery Efforts for Edo Period Artifacts," ''Asahi Shimbun'' (February 8, 2023).</ref>
== area == The dimensions of the mound are as follows:<ref name="OhtawaraCity"/><ref name="descriptionboard"/> * '''Mound length:''' 114 meters * '''Posterior section:''' ** Length: 60.5 meters ** Width: 58 meters ** Height: 11.5 meters * '''Anterior section:''' ** Length: 53.5 meters ** Width: 52 meters ** Height: 6.5 meters
The surrounding moat is visible on the north, west, and south sides, while the east side features a natural slope leading to the Naka River.
== Historical Significance == Kamisamuraizuka Kofun, along with Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, is celebrated as the site of Japan's first academically documented archaeological excavation. Under Tokugawa Mitsukuni's orders, the excavation aimed to explore and preserve the cultural heritage of the Nasu region. The discovery of artifacts, followed by their careful documentation and reburial, marks a significant milestone in Japanese archaeology and cultural preservation.<ref name="Asahi20230208"/>
The site was designated a National Historic Site in 1951 as part of the "Samuraizuka Kofun" complex.<ref name="NationalHistoricSite">{{Cite database|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/401/483|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs}}.</ref>
== Related Sites == * '''Shimosamuraizuka Kofun:''' Located nearby, this zenpō-kōhō-fun complements Kamisamuraizuka in historical and archaeological importance. * '''Kamisamuraizuka North Kofun:''' A smaller zenpō-kōhō-fun situated north of Kamisamuraizuka. Little is known about this kofun due to the lack of detailed investigations.

'''Kamisamuraizuka Kofun''' (上侍塚古墳) is a {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} located in {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市}}, {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, Japan. Together with {{ill|Shimosamuraizuka Kofun|ja|下侍塚古墳}}, it is part of the National Historic Site "Samuraizuka Kofun." This kofun is notable for being one of the first to undergo a scholarly excavation during the Edo period under the orders of {{ill|Tokugawa Mitsukuni|ja|徳川光圀}}, known as "Mito Kōmon."
== Overview == Kamisamuraizuka Kofun is a large zenpō-kōhō-fun constructed on the right bank of the {{ill|Naka River|ja|那珂川}}. Alongside Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, it represents one of the most significant ancient Kofuns in the Nasu region. Excavations date back to the 5th year of the Genroku era (1692), and further archaeological surveys were conducted starting in 2021.
The mound faces south, with a total length of 114 meters, making it the largest zenpō-kōhō-fun in the Nasu area and the second largest in Tochigi Prefecture after the {{ill|Fujimoto Kannonyama Kofun|ja|藤本観音山古墳}} in {{ill|Ashikaga|ja|足利市}}.<ref name="OhtawaraCity">[https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082767660/ Samuraizuka Kofun] (Ōtawara City website).</ref> The mound is covered with fukiishi (paving stones) and surrounded by a moat approximately 20 meters wide. The burial chamber is believed to have been constructed with clay.<ref name="descriptionboard">Site explanation board.</ref>
Artifacts recovered during the Edo period include bronze mirrors, tubular beads, stone armlets, and iron objects such as armor fragments. These items were reburied in a wooden box following the excavation.<ref name="Asahi20230208">"Rediscovery Efforts for Edo Period Artifacts," ''Asahi Shimbun'' (February 8, 2023).</ref>
== area == The dimensions of the mound are as follows:<ref name="OhtawaraCity"/><ref name="descriptionboard"/> * '''Mound length:''' 114 meters * '''Posterior section:''' ** Length: 60.5 meters ** Width: 58 meters ** Height: 11.5 meters * '''Anterior section:''' ** Length: 53.5 meters ** Width: 52 meters ** Height: 6.5 meters
The surrounding moat is visible on the north, west, and south sides, while the east side features a natural slope leading to the Naka River.
== Historical Significance == Kamisamuraizuka Kofun, along with Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, is celebrated as the site of Japan's first academically documented archaeological excavation. Under Tokugawa Mitsukuni's orders, the excavation aimed to explore and preserve the cultural heritage of the Nasu region. The discovery of artifacts, followed by their careful documentation and reburial, marks a significant milestone in Japanese archaeology and cultural preservation.<ref name="Asahi20230208"/>
The site was designated a National Historic Site in 1951 as part of the "Samuraizuka Kofun" complex.<ref name="NationalHistoricSite">{{Cite database|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/401/483|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs}}.</ref>
== Related Sites == * '''Shimosamuraizuka Kofun:''' Located nearby, this zenpō-kōhō-fun complements Kamisamuraizuka in historical and archaeological importance. * '''Kamisamuraizuka North Kofun:''' A smaller zenpō-kōhō-fun situated north of Kamisamuraizuka. Little is known about this kofun due to the lack of detailed investigations.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Further Reading == * [https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/115806 "Kamisamuraizuka Kofun Excavation Reports"] (Tochigi Future Creation Foundation, 2021). * [https://kotobank.jp/word/%E4%BE%8D%E5%A1%9A%E5%8F%A4%E5%A2%B3-69803 "Samuraizuka Kofun"] - Kotobank entry.

'''Kamisamuraizuka Kofun''' (上侍塚古墳) is a {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} located in {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市}}, {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, Japan. Together with {{ill|Shimosamuraizuka Kofun|ja|下侍塚古墳}}, it is part of the National other_designation "Samuraizuka Kofun." This kofun is notable for being one of the first to undergo a scholarly excavation during the Edo period under the orders of {{ill|Tokugawa Mitsukuni|ja|徳川光圀}}, known as "Mito Kōmon."
== Overview == Kamisamuraizuka Kofun is a large zenpō-kōhō-fun constructed on the right bank of the {{ill|Naka River|ja|那珂川}}. Alongside Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, it represents one of the most significant ancient Kofuns in the Nasu region. Excavations date back to the 5th year of the Genroku era (1692), and further archaeological surveys were conducted starting in 2021.
The mound faces south, with a total length of 114 meters, making it the largest zenpō-kōhō-fun in the Nasu area and the second largest in Tochigi Prefecture after the {{ill|Fujimoto Kannonyama Kofun|ja|藤本観音山古墳}} in {{ill|Ashikaga|ja|足利市}}.<ref name="OhtawaraCity">[https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082767660/ Samuraizuka Kofun] (Ōtawara City website).</ref> The mound is covered with fukiishi (paving stones) and surrounded by a moat approximately 20 meters wide. The burial chamber is believed to have been constructed with clay.<ref name="descriptionboard">Site explanation board.</ref>
Artifacts recovered during the Edo period include bronze mirrors, tubular beads, stone armlets, and iron objects such as armor fragments. These items were reburied in a wooden box following the excavation.<ref name="Asahi20230208">"Rediscovery Efforts for Edo Period Artifacts," ''Asahi Shimbun'' (February 8, 2023).</ref>
== area == The dimensions of the mound are as follows:<ref name="OhtawaraCity"/><ref name="descriptionboard"/> * '''Mound length:''' 114 meters * '''Posterior section:''' ** Length: 60.5 meters ** Width: 58 meters ** Height: 11.5 meters * '''Anterior section:''' ** Length: 53.5 meters ** Width: 52 meters ** Height: 6.5 meters
The surrounding moat is visible on the north, west, and south sides, while the east side features a natural slope leading to the Naka River.
== Historical Significance == Kamisamuraizuka Kofun, along with Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, is celebrated as the site of Japan's first academically documented archaeological excavation. Under Tokugawa Mitsukuni's orders, the excavation aimed to explore and preserve the cultural heritage of the Nasu region. The discovery of artifacts, followed by their careful documentation and reburial, marks a significant milestone in Japanese archaeology and cultural preservation.<ref name="Asahi20230208"/>
The site was designated a National other_designation in 1951 as part of the "Samuraizuka Kofun" complex.<ref name="NationalHistoricSite">{{Cite database|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/401/483|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs}}.</ref>
== Related Sites == * '''Shimosamuraizuka Kofun:''' Located nearby, this zenpō-kōhō-fun complements Kamisamuraizuka in historical and archaeological importance. * '''Kamisamuraizuka North Kofun:''' A smaller zenpō-kōhō-fun situated north of Kamisamuraizuka. Little is known about this kofun due to the lack of detailed investigations.
