Please do not use any bots that just remove the unknown parameters without looking at them. This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun}} |name = Kamisamuraizuka Kofun |image = [[File:Kamisamuraizuka Kofun, funkyu-2.jpg|280px]] |Caption = Mound (left: posterior section, right: anterior section) |alternate_name = Kurumazuka | part_of = |location = {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市}}, {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, Japan |coordinates = {{coord|36|48|21.05|N|140|7|29.45|E|region:JP-09_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |type = {{ill|Zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} |area = Mound length: 114m, height: 11.5m (posterior section) |Burial = Clay chamber |burial goods = Bronze mirror, tubular beads, stone armlets, iron objects, haji pottery |Subordinate Tombs = |Era = Late 4th century |Buried Person = |Mausoleum = |alternate_name = Included in the National other_designation "Samuraizuka Kofun" |Cultural Asset Designation = |notes = Second largest zenpō-kōhō-fun in Tochigi Prefecture |map = Japan Tochigi |type = Zenpō-kōhō-fun-S |map_dot_label = Kamisamuraizuka Kofun |map_dot_label = top }}
'''Kamisamuraizuka Kofun''' (上侍塚古墳) is a {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} located in {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市}}, {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, Japan. Together with {{ill|Shimosamuraizuka Kofun|ja|下侍塚古墳}}, it is part of the National other_designation "Samuraizuka Kofun." This kofun is notable for being one of the first to undergo a scholarly excavation during the Edo period under the orders of {{ill|Tokugawa Mitsukuni|ja|徳川光圀}}, known as "Mito Kōmon."
== Overview == Kamisamuraizuka Kofun is a large zenpō-kōhō-fun constructed on the right bank of the {{ill|Naka River|ja|那珂川}}. Alongside Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, it represents one of the most significant ancient Kofuns in the Nasu region. Excavations date back to the 5th year of the Genroku era (1692), and further archaeological surveys were conducted starting in 2021.
The mound faces south, with a total length of 114 meters, making it the largest zenpō-kōhō-fun in the Nasu area and the second largest in Tochigi Prefecture after the {{ill|Fujimoto Kannonyama Kofun|ja|藤本観音山古墳}} in {{ill|Ashikaga|ja|足利市}}.<ref name="OhtawaraCity">[https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082767660/ Samuraizuka Kofun] (Ōtawara City website).</ref> The mound is covered with fukiishi (paving stones) and surrounded by a moat approximately 20 meters wide. The burial chamber is believed to have been constructed with clay.<ref name="descriptionboard">Site explanation board.</ref>
burial goods recovered during the Edo period include bronze mirrors, tubular beads, stone armlets, and iron objects such as armor fragments. These items were reburied in a wooden box following the excavation.<ref name="Asahi20230208">"Rediscovery Efforts for Edo Period burial goods," ''Asahi Shimbun'' (February 8, 2023).</ref>
== area == The dimensions of the mound are as follows:<ref name="OhtawaraCity"/><ref name="descriptionboard"/> * '''Mound length:''' 114 meters * '''Posterior section:''' ** Length: 60.5 meters ** Width: 58 meters ** Height: 11.5 meters * '''Anterior section:''' ** Length: 53.5 meters ** Width: 52 meters ** Height: 6.5 meters
The surrounding moat is visible on the north, west, and south sides, while the east side features a natural slope leading to the Naka River.
== Historical Significance == Kamisamuraizuka Kofun, along with Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, is celebrated as the site of Japan's first academically documented archaeological excavation. Under Tokugawa Mitsukuni's orders, the excavation aimed to explore and preserve the cultural heritage of the Nasu region. The discovery of burial goods, followed by their careful documentation and reburial, marks a significant milestone in Japanese archaeology and cultural preservation.<ref name="Asahi20230208"/>
The site was designation1 a National other_designation in 1951 as part of the "Samuraizuka Kofun" complex.<ref name="NationalHistoricSite">{{Cite database|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/401/483|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs}}.</ref>
== Related Sites == * '''Shimosamuraizuka Kofun:''' Located nearby, this zenpō-kōhō-fun complements Kamisamuraizuka in historical and archaeological importance. * '''Kamisamuraizuka North Kofun:''' A smaller zenpō-kōhō-fun situated north of Kamisamuraizuka. Little is known about this kofun due to the lack of detailed investigations.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Further Reading == * [https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/115806 "Kamisamuraizuka Kofun Excavation Reports"] (Tochigi Future Creation Foundation, 2021). * [https://kotobank.jp/word/%E4%BE%8D%E5%A1%9A%E5%8F%A4%E5%A2%B3-69803 "Samuraizuka Kofun"] - Kotobank entry.
== External Links == * {{Cite database|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/401/483|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs}} * [https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082767660/ Samuraizuka Kofun] - Ōtawara City website.

{{afc comment|1=trimming these off. the parameter works as "burial" so no need to use "Burial Facility"[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:35, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=added parameters Interred_individual and Mausoleum is planned to be added but I messed up in template editing[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:40, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=now we have a 'burial' and 'burial goods' parameter. So a lot of these pages might not be marked on our wiki as having unknown parameters. But specifically keeping this comment here in case of import, these parameters should be added to [[Template:infobox ancient site]] rather than deleted from this draft or article[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:01, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=I hope this is not too annoying but this article is gonna be placed under [[:Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] and there is a decent chance these unknown parameters are ones[[Category:marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] I want added to the main template like burial chamber structure and burial goods. Please do not use any bots that just remove the unknown parameters without looking at them. This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun}} |name = Kamisamuraizuka Kofun |image = [[File:Kamisamuraizuka Kofun, funkyu-2.jpg|280px]] |Caption = Mound (left: posterior section, right: anterior section) |alternate_name = Kurumazuka | part_of = |location = {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市}}, {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, Japan |coordinates = {{coord|36|48|21.05|N|140|7|29.45|E|region:JP-09_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |type = {{ill|Zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} |area = Mound length: 114m, height: 11.5m (posterior section) |Burial = Clay chamber |burial goods = Bronze mirror, tubular beads, stone armlets, iron objects, haji pottery |Subordinate Tombs = |Era = Late 4th century |Buried Person = |Mausoleum = |alternate_name = Included in the National other_designation "Samuraizuka Kofun" |Cultural Asset Designation = |notes = Second largest zenpō-kōhō-fun in Tochigi Prefecture |map = Japan Tochigi |type = Zenpō-kōhō-fun-S |map_dot_label = Kamisamuraizuka Kofun |map_dot_label = top }}
'''Kamisamuraizuka Kofun''' (上侍塚古墳) is a {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} located in {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市}}, {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, Japan. Together with {{ill|Shimosamuraizuka Kofun|ja|下侍塚古墳}}, it is part of the National other_designation "Samuraizuka Kofun." This kofun is notable for being one of the first to undergo a scholarly excavation during the Edo period under the orders of {{ill|Tokugawa Mitsukuni|ja|徳川光圀}}, known as "Mito Kōmon."
== Overview == Kamisamuraizuka Kofun is a large zenpō-kōhō-fun constructed on the right bank of the {{ill|Naka River|ja|那珂川}}. Alongside Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, it represents one of the most significant ancient Kofuns in the Nasu region. Excavations date back to the 5th year of the Genroku era (1692), and further archaeological surveys were conducted starting in 2021.
The mound faces south, with a total length of 114 meters, making it the largest zenpō-kōhō-fun in the Nasu area and the second largest in Tochigi Prefecture after the {{ill|Fujimoto Kannonyama Kofun|ja|藤本観音山古墳}} in {{ill|Ashikaga|ja|足利市}}.<ref name="OhtawaraCity">[https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082767660/ Samuraizuka Kofun] (Ōtawara City website).</ref> The mound is covered with fukiishi (paving stones) and surrounded by a moat approximately 20 meters wide. The burial chamber is believed to have been constructed with clay.<ref name="descriptionboard">Site explanation board.</ref>
burial goods recovered during the Edo period include bronze mirrors, tubular beads, stone armlets, and iron objects such as armor fragments. These items were reburied in a wooden box following the excavation.<ref name="Asahi20230208">"Rediscovery Efforts for Edo Period burial goods," ''Asahi Shimbun'' (February 8, 2023).</ref>
== area == The area of the mound are as follows:<ref name="OhtawaraCity"/><ref name="descriptionboard"/> * '''Mound length:''' 114 meters * '''Posterior section:''' ** Length: 60.5 meters ** Width: 58 meters ** Height: 11.5 meters * '''Anterior section:''' ** Length: 53.5 meters ** Width: 52 meters ** Height: 6.5 meters
The surrounding moat is visible on the north, west, and south sides, while the east side features a natural slope leading to the Naka River.
== Historical Significance == Kamisamuraizuka Kofun, along with Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, is celebrated as the site of Japan's first academically documented archaeological excavation. Under Tokugawa Mitsukuni's orders, the excavation aimed to explore and preserve the cultural heritage of the Nasu region. The discovery of burial goods, followed by their careful documentation and reburial, marks a significant milestone in Japanese archaeology and cultural preservation.<ref name="Asahi20230208"/>
The site was designation1 a National other_designation in 1951 as part of the "Samuraizuka Kofun" complex.<ref name="NationalHistoricSite">{{Cite database|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/401/483|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs}}.</ref>
== Related Sites == * '''Shimosamuraizuka Kofun:''' Located nearby, this zenpō-kōhō-fun complements Kamisamuraizuka in historical and archaeological importance. * '''Kamisamuraizuka North Kofun:''' A smaller zenpō-kōhō-fun situated north of Kamisamuraizuka. Little is known about this kofun due to the lack of detailed investigations.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Further Reading == * [https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/115806 "Kamisamuraizuka Kofun Excavation Reports"] (Tochigi Future Creation Foundation, 2021). * [https://kotobank.jp/word/%E4%BE%8D%E5%A1%9A%E5%8F%A4%E5%A2%B3-69803 "Samuraizuka Kofun"] - Kotobank entry.
== External Links == * {{Cite database|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/401/483|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs}} * [https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082767660/ Samuraizuka Kofun] - Ōtawara City website.

The site was other_designation1 a National other_other_designation in 1951 as part of the "Samuraizuka Kofun" complex.<ref name="NationalHistoricSite">{{Cite database|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/401/483|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs}}.</ref>

'''Kamisamuraizuka Kofun''' (上侍塚古墳) is a {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} located in {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市}}, {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, Japan. Together with {{ill|Shimosamuraizuka Kofun|ja|下侍塚古墳}}, it is part of the National other_other_designation "Samuraizuka Kofun." This kofun is notable for being one of the first to undergo a scholarly excavation during the Edo period under the orders of {{ill|Tokugawa Mitsukuni|ja|徳川光圀}}, known as "Mito Kōmon."
== Overview == Kamisamuraizuka Kofun is a large zenpō-kōhō-fun constructed on the right bank of the {{ill|Naka River|ja|那珂川}}. Alongside Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, it represents one of the most significant ancient Kofuns in the Nasu region. Excavations date back to the 5th year of the Genroku era (1692), and further archaeological surveys were conducted starting in 2021.
The mound faces south, with a total length of 114 meters, making it the largest zenpō-kōhō-fun in the Nasu area and the second largest in Tochigi Prefecture after the {{ill|Fujimoto Kannonyama Kofun|ja|藤本観音山古墳}} in {{ill|Ashikaga|ja|足利市}}.<ref name="OhtawaraCity">[https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082767660/ Samuraizuka Kofun] (Ōtawara City website).</ref> The mound is covered with fukiishi (paving stones) and surrounded by a moat approximately 20 meters wide. The burial chamber is believed to have been constructed with clay.<ref name="descriptionboard">Site explanation board.</ref>
burial goods recovered during the Edo period include bronze mirrors, tubular beads, stone armlets, and iron objects such as armor fragments. These items were reburied in a wooden box following the excavation.<ref name="Asahi20230208">"Rediscovery Efforts for Edo Period burial goods," ''Asahi Shimbun'' (February 8, 2023).</ref>
== area == The area of the mound are as follows:<ref name="OhtawaraCity"/><ref name="descriptionboard"/> * '''Mound length:''' 114 meters * '''Posterior section:''' ** Length: 60.5 meters ** Width: 58 meters ** Height: 11.5 meters * '''Anterior section:''' ** Length: 53.5 meters ** Width: 52 meters ** Height: 6.5 meters
The surrounding moat is visible on the north, west, and south sides, while the east side features a natural slope leading to the Naka River.
== Historical Significance == Kamisamuraizuka Kofun, along with Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, is celebrated as the site of Japan's first academically documented archaeological excavation. Under Tokugawa Mitsukuni's orders, the excavation aimed to explore and preserve the cultural heritage of the Nasu region. The discovery of burial goods, followed by their careful documentation and reburial, marks a significant milestone in Japanese archaeology and cultural preservation.<ref name="Asahi20230208"/>
The site was other_designation1 a National other_other_designation in 1951 as part of the "Samuraizuka Kofun" complex.<ref name="NationalHistoricSite">{{Cite database|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/401/483|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs}}.</ref>
== Related Sites == * '''Shimosamuraizuka Kofun:''' Located nearby, this zenpō-kōhō-fun complements Kamisamuraizuka in historical and archaeological importance. * '''Kamisamuraizuka North Kofun:''' A smaller zenpō-kōhō-fun situated north of Kamisamuraizuka. Little is known about this kofun due to the lack of detailed investigations.

Under Tokugawa Mitsukuni's orders, the excavation aimed to explore and preserve the cultural heritage of the Nasu region. The discovery of burial goods, followed by their careful documentation and reburial, marks a significant milestone in Japanese archaeology and cultural preservation.<ref name="Asahi20230208"/>
The site was other_designation1 a National other_designation in 1951 as part of the "Samuraizuka Kofun" complex.<ref name="NationalHistoricSite">{{Cite database|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/401/483|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs}}.</ref>
== Related Sites == * '''Shimosamuraizuka Kofun:''' Located nearby, this zenpō-kōhō-fun complements Kamisamuraizuka in historical and archaeological importance. * '''Kamisamuraizuka North Kofun:''' A smaller zenpō-kōhō-fun situated north of Kamisamuraizuka. Little is known about this kofun due to the lack of detailed investigations.

'''Kamisamuraizuka Kofun''' (上侍塚古墳) is a {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} located in {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市}}, {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, Japan. Together with {{ill|Shimosamuraizuka Kofun|ja|下侍塚古墳}}, it is part of the National other_designation "Samuraizuka Kofun." This kofun is notable for being one of the first to undergo a scholarly excavation during the Edo period under the orders of {{ill|Tokugawa Mitsukuni|ja|徳川光圀}}, known as "Mito Kōmon."
== Overview == Kamisamuraizuka Kofun is a large zenpō-kōhō-fun constructed on the right bank of the {{ill|Naka River|ja|那珂川}}. Alongside Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, it represents one of the most significant ancient Kofuns in the Nasu region. Excavations date back to the 5th year of the Genroku era (1692), and further archaeological surveys were conducted starting in 2021.
The mound faces south, with a total length of 114 meters, making it the largest zenpō-kōhō-fun in the Nasu area and the second largest in Tochigi Prefecture after the {{ill|Fujimoto Kannonyama Kofun|ja|藤本観音山古墳}} in {{ill|Ashikaga|ja|足利市}}.<ref name="OhtawaraCity">[https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082767660/ Samuraizuka Kofun] (Ōtawara City website).</ref> The mound is covered with fukiishi (paving stones) and surrounded by a moat approximately 20 meters wide. The burial chamber is believed to have been constructed with clay.<ref name="descriptionboard">Site explanation board.</ref>
burial goods recovered during the Edo period include bronze mirrors, tubular beads, stone armlets, and iron objects such as armor fragments. These items were reburied in a wooden box following the excavation.<ref name="Asahi20230208">"Rediscovery Efforts for Edo Period burial goods," ''Asahi Shimbun'' (February 8, 2023).</ref>
== area == The area of the mound are as follows:<ref name="OhtawaraCity"/><ref name="descriptionboard"/> * '''Mound length:''' 114 meters * '''Posterior section:''' ** Length: 60.5 meters ** Width: 58 meters ** Height: 11.5 meters * '''Anterior section:''' ** Length: 53.5 meters ** Width: 52 meters ** Height: 6.5 meters
The surrounding moat is visible on the north, west, and south sides, while the east side features a natural slope leading to the Naka River.
== Historical Significance == Kamisamuraizuka Kofun, along with Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, is celebrated as the site of Japan's first academically documented archaeological excavation. Under Tokugawa Mitsukuni's orders, the excavation aimed to explore and preserve the cultural heritage of the Nasu region. The discovery of burial goods, followed by their careful documentation and reburial, marks a significant milestone in Japanese archaeology and cultural preservation.<ref name="Asahi20230208"/>
The site was other_designation1 a National other_designation in 1951 as part of the "Samuraizuka Kofun" complex.<ref name="Nationalother_designation">{{Cite database|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/401/483|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs}}.</ref>
== Related Sites == * '''Shimosamuraizuka Kofun:''' Located nearby, this zenpō-kōhō-fun complements Kamisamuraizuka in historical and archaeological importance. * '''Kamisamuraizuka North Kofun:''' A smaller zenpō-kōhō-fun situated north of Kamisamuraizuka. Little is known about this kofun due to the lack of detailed investigations.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Further Reading == * [https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/115806 "Kamisamuraizuka Kofun Excavation Reports"] (Tochigi Future Creation Foundation, 2021). * [https://kotobank.jp/word/%E4%BE%8D%E5%A1%9A%E5%8F%A4%E5%A2%B3-69803 "Samuraizuka Kofun"] - Kotobank entry.
== External Links == * {{Cite database|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/401/483|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs}} * [https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082767660/ Samuraizuka Kofun] - Ōtawara City website.

These items were reburied in a wooden box following the excavation.<ref name="Asahi20230208">"Rediscovery Efforts for Edo Period burial goods," ''Asahi Shimbun'' (February 8, 2023).</ref>
== area == The area of the mound are as follows:<ref name="OhtawaraCity"/><ref name="descriptionboard"/> * '''Mound length:''' 114 meters * '''Posterior section:''' ** Length: 60.5 meters ** Width: 58 meters ** Height: 11.5 meters * '''Anterior section:''' ** Length: 53.5 meters ** Width: 52 meters ** Height: 6.5 meters
The surrounding moat is visible on the north, west, and south sides, while the east side features a natural slope leading to the Naka River.
== Historical Significance == Kamisamuraizuka Kofun, along with Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, is celebrated as the site of Japan's first academically documented archaeological excavation. Under Tokugawa Mitsukuni's orders, the excavation aimed to explore and preserve the cultural heritage of the Nasu region. The discovery of burial goods, followed by their careful documentation and reburial, marks a significant milestone in Japanese archaeology and cultural preservation.<ref name="Asahi20230208"/>
The site was other_designation1 a National other_designation in 1951 as part of the "Samuraizuka Kofun" complex.<ref name="Nationalother_designation">{{Cite database|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/401/483|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs}}.</ref>
== Related Sites == * '''Shimosamuraizuka Kofun:''' Located nearby, this zenpō-kōhō-fun complements Kamisamuraizuka in historical and archaeological importance. * '''Kamisamuraizuka North Kofun:''' A smaller zenpō-kōhō-fun situated north of Kamisamuraizuka. Little is known about this kofun due to the lack of detailed investigations.

'''Kamisamuraizuka Kofun''' (上侍塚古墳) is a {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} located in {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市}}, {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, Japan. Together with {{ill|Shimosamuraizuka Kofun|ja|下侍塚古墳}}, it is part of the National other_designation "Samuraizuka Kofun." This kofun is notable for being one of the first to undergo a scholarly excavation during the Edo period under the orders of {{ill|Tokugawa Mitsukuni|ja|徳川光圀}}, known as "Mito Kōmon."
== Overview == Kamisamuraizuka Kofun is a large zenpō-kōhō-fun constructed on the right bank of the {{ill|Naka River|ja|那珂川}}. Alongside Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, it represents one of the most significant ancient Kofuns in the Nasu region. Excavations date back to the 5th year of the Genroku era (1692), and further archaeological surveys were conducted starting in 2021.
The mound faces south, with a total length of 114 meters, making it the largest zenpō-kōhō-fun in the Nasu area and the second largest in Tochigi Prefecture after the {{ill|Fujimoto Kannonyama Kofun|ja|藤本観音山古墳}} in {{ill|Ashikaga|ja|足利市}}.<ref name="OhtawaraCity">[https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082767660/ Samuraizuka Kofun] (Ōtawara City website).</ref> The mound is covered with fukiishi (paving stones) and surrounded by a moat approximately 20 meters wide. The burial chamber is believed to have been constructed with clay.<ref name="descriptionboard">Site explanation board.</ref>
burial goods recovered during the Edo period include bronze mirrors, tubular beads, stone armlets, and iron objects such as armor fragments. These items were reburied in a wooden box following the excavation.<ref name="Asahi20230208">"Rediscovery Efforts for Edo Period burial goods," ''Asahi Shimbun'' (February 8, 2023).</ref>
== area == The area of the mound are as follows:<ref name="OhtawaraCity"/><ref name="descriptionboard"/> * '''Mound length:''' 114 meters * '''Posterior section:''' ** Length: 60.5 meters ** Width: 58 meters ** Height: 11.5 meters * '''Anterior section:''' ** Length: 53.5 meters ** Width: 52 meters ** Height: 6.5 meters
The surrounding moat is visible on the north, west, and south sides, while the east side features a natural slope leading to the Naka River.
== Historical Significance == Kamisamuraizuka Kofun, along with Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, is celebrated as the site of Japan's first academically documented archaeological excavation. Under Tokugawa Mitsukuni's orders, the excavation aimed to explore and preserve the cultural heritage of the Nasu region. The discovery of burial goods, followed by their careful documentation and reburial, marks a significant milestone in Japanese archaeology and cultural preservation.<ref name="Asahi20230208"/>
The site was other_designation1 a National other_designation in 1951 as part of the "Samuraizuka Kofun" complex.<ref name="Nationalother_designation">{{Cite database|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/401/483|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs}}.</ref>
== Related Sites == * '''Shimosamuraizuka Kofun:''' Located nearby, this zenpō-kōhō-fun complements Kamisamuraizuka in historical and archaeological importance. * '''Kamisamuraizuka North Kofun:''' A smaller zenpō-kōhō-fun situated north of Kamisamuraizuka. Little is known about this kofun due to the lack of detailed investigations.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Further Reading == * [https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/115806 "Kamisamuraizuka Kofun Excavation Reports"] (Tochigi Future Creation Foundation, 2021). * [https://kotobank.jp/word/%E4%BE%8D%E5%A1%9A%E5%8F%A4%E5%A2%B3-69803 "Samuraizuka Kofun"] - Kotobank entry.

== Overview == Kamisamuraizuka Kofun is a large zenpō-kōhō-fun constructed on the right bank of the {{ill|Naka River|ja|那珂川}}. Alongside Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, it represents one of the most significant ancient Kofuns in the Nasu region. Excavations date back to the 5th year of the Genroku era (1692), and further archaeological surveys were conducted starting in 2021.
The mound faces south, with a total length of 114 meters, making it the largest zenpō-kōhō-fun in the Nasu area and the second largest in Tochigi Prefecture after the {{ill|Fujimoto Kannonyama Kofun|ja|藤本観音山古墳}} in {{ill|Ashikaga|ja|足利市}}.<ref name="OhtawaraCity">[https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082767660/ Samuraizuka Kofun] (Ōtawara City website).</ref> The mound is covered with fukiishi (paving stones) and surrounded by a moat approximately 20 meters wide. The burial chamber is believed to have been constructed with clay.<ref name="descriptionboard">Site explanation board.</ref>
burial goods recovered during the Edo period include bronze mirrors, tubular beads, stone armlets, and iron objects such as armor fragments. These items were reburied in a wooden box following the excavation.<ref name="Asahi20230208">"Rediscovery Efforts for Edo Period burial goods," ''Asahi Shimbun'' (February 8, 2023).</ref>
== area == The area of the mound are as follows:<ref name="OhtawaraCity"/><ref name="descriptionboard"/> * '''Mound length:''' 114 meters * '''Posterior section:''' ** Length: 60.5 meters ** Width: 58 meters ** Height: 11.5 meters * '''Anterior section:''' ** Length: 53.5 meters ** Width: 52 meters ** Height: 6.5 meters
The surrounding moat is visible on the north, west, and south sides, while the east side features a natural slope leading to the Naka River.
== Historical Significance == Kamisamuraizuka Kofun, along with Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, is celebrated as the site of Japan's first academically documented archaeological excavation. Under Tokugawa Mitsukuni's orders, the excavation aimed to explore and preserve the cultural heritage of the Nasu region. The discovery of burial goods, followed by their careful documentation and reburial, marks a significant milestone in Japanese archaeology and cultural preservation.<ref name="Asahi20230208"/>
The site was other_designation1 a National other_designation in 1951 as part of the "Samuraizuka Kofun" complex.<ref name="Nationalother_designation">{{Cite database|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/401/483|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs}}.</ref>
== Related Sites == * '''Shimosamuraizuka Kofun:''' Located nearby, this zenpō-kōhō-fun complements Kamisamuraizuka in historical and archaeological importance. * '''Kamisamuraizuka North Kofun:''' A smaller zenpō-kōhō-fun situated north of Kamisamuraizuka. Little is known about this kofun due to the lack of detailed investigations.

== Further Reading == * [https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/115806 "Kamisamuraizuka Kofun Excavation Reports"] (Tochigi Future Creation Foundation, 2021). * [https://kotobank.jp/word/%E4%BE%8D%E5%A1%9A%E5%8F%A4%E5%A2%B3-69803 "Samuraizuka Kofun"] - Kotobank entry.

'''Kamisamuraizuka Kofun''' (上侍塚古墳) is a {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} located in {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市}}, {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, Japan. Together with {{ill|Shimosamuraizuka Kofun|ja|下侍塚古墳}}, it is part of the National other_designation "Samuraizuka Kofun." This kofun is notable for being one of the first to undergo a scholarly excavation during the Edo period under the orders of {{ill|Tokugawa Mitsukuni|ja|徳川光圀}}, known as "Mito Kōmon."

'''Kamisamuraizuka Kofun''' (上侍塚古墳) is a {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} located in {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市}}, {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, Japan. Together with {{ill|Shimosamuraizuka Kofun|ja|下侍塚古墳}}, it is part of the National other_designation "Samuraizuka Kofun." This kofun is notable for being one of the first to undergo a scholarly excavation during the Edo period under the orders of {{ill|Tokugawa Mitsukuni|ja|徳川光圀}}, known as "Mito Kōmon."
== Overview == Kamisamuraizuka Kofun is a large zenpō-kōhō-fun constructed on the right bank of the {{ill|Naka River|ja|那珂川}}. Alongside Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, it represents one of the most significant ancient Kofuns in the Nasu region. Excavations date back to the 5th year of the Genroku era (1692), and further archaeological surveys were conducted starting in 2021.
The mound faces south, with a total length of 114 meters, making it the largest zenpō-kōhō-fun in the Nasu area and the second largest in Tochigi Prefecture after the {{ill|Fujimoto Kannonyama Kofun|ja|藤本観音山古墳}} in {{ill|Ashikaga|ja|足利市}}.<ref name="OhtawaraCity">[https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082767660/ Samuraizuka Kofun] (Ōtawara City website).</ref> The mound is covered with fukiishi (paving stones) and surrounded by a moat approximately 20 meters wide. The burial chamber is believed to have been constructed with clay.<ref name="descriptionboard">Site explanation board.</ref>
burial goods recovered during the Edo period include bronze mirrors, tubular beads, stone armlets, and iron objects such as armor fragments. These items were reburied in a wooden box following the excavation.<ref name="Asahi20230208">"Rediscovery Efforts for Edo Period burial goods," ''Asahi Shimbun'' (February 8, 2023).</ref>
== area == The area of the mound are as follows:<ref name="OhtawaraCity"/><ref name="descriptionboard"/> * '''Mound length:''' 114 meters * '''Posterior section:''' ** Length: 60.5 meters ** Width: 58 meters ** Height: 11.5 meters * '''Anterior section:''' ** Length: 53.5 meters ** Width: 52 meters ** Height: 6.5 meters
The surrounding moat is visible on the north, west, and south sides, while the east side features a natural slope leading to the Naka River.
== Historical Significance == Kamisamuraizuka Kofun, along with Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, is celebrated as the site of Japan's first academically documented archaeological excavation. Under Tokugawa Mitsukuni's orders, the excavation aimed to explore and preserve the cultural heritage of the Nasu region. The discovery of burial goods, followed by their careful documentation and reburial, marks a significant milestone in Japanese archaeology and cultural preservation.<ref name="Asahi20230208"/>
The site was other_designation1 a National other_designation in 1951 as part of the "Samuraizuka Kofun" complex.<ref name="Nationalother_designation">{{Cite database|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/401/483|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs}}.</ref>
== Related Sites == * '''Shimosamuraizuka Kofun:''' Located nearby, this zenpō-kōhō-fun complements Kamisamuraizuka in historical and archaeological importance. * '''Kamisamuraizuka North Kofun:''' A smaller zenpō-kōhō-fun situated north of Kamisamuraizuka. Little is known about this kofun due to the lack of detailed investigations.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Further Reading == * [https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/115806 "Kamisamuraizuka Kofun Excavation Reports"] (Tochigi Future Creation Foundation, 2021). * [https://kotobank.jp/word/%E4%BE%8D%E5%A1%9A%E5%8F%A4%E5%A2%B3-69803 "Samuraizuka Kofun"] - Kotobank entry.
== External Links == * {{Cite database|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/401/483|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs}} * [https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082767660/ Samuraizuka Kofun] - Ōtawara City website.

'''Kamisamuraizuka Kofun''' (上侍塚古墳) is a {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} located in {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市}}, {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, Japan. Together with {{ill|Shimosamuraizuka Kofun|ja|下侍塚古墳}}, it is part of the National other_designation "Samuraizuka Kofun." This kofun is notable for being one of the first to undergo a scholarly excavation during the Edo period under the orders of {{ill|Tokugawa Mitsukuni|ja|徳川光圀}}, known as "Mito Kōmon."
== Overview == Kamisamuraizuka Kofun is a large zenpō-kōhō-fun constructed on the right bank of the {{ill|Naka River|ja|那珂川}}. Alongside Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, it represents one of the most significant ancient Kofuns in the Nasu region. Excavations date back to the 5th year of the Genroku era (1692), and further archaeological surveys were conducted starting in 2021.
The mound faces south, with a total length of 114 meters, making it the largest zenpō-kōhō-fun in the Nasu area and the second largest in Tochigi Prefecture after the {{ill|Fujimoto Kannonyama Kofun|ja|藤本観音山古墳}} in {{ill|Ashikaga|ja|足利市}}.<ref name="OhtawaraCity">[https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082767660/ Samuraizuka Kofun] (Ōtawara City website).</ref> The mound is covered with fukiishi (paving stones) and surrounded by a moat approximately 20 meters wide. The burial chamber is believed to have been constructed with clay.<ref name="descriptionboard">Site explanation board.</ref>
burial goods recovered during the Edo period include bronze mirrors, tubular beads, stone armlets, and iron objects such as armor fragments. These items were reburied in a wooden box following the excavation.<ref name="Asahi20230208">"Rediscovery Efforts for Edo built burial goods," ''Asahi Shimbun'' (February 8, 2023).</ref>
== area == The area of the mound are as follows:<ref name="OhtawaraCity"/><ref name="descriptionboard"/> * '''Mound length:''' 114 meters * '''Posterior section:''' ** Length: 60.5 meters ** Width: 58 meters ** Height: 11.5 meters * '''Anterior section:''' ** Length: 53.5 meters ** Width: 52 meters ** Height: 6.5 meters
The surrounding moat is visible on the north, west, and south sides, while the east side features a natural slope leading to the Naka River.
== Historical Significance == Kamisamuraizuka Kofun, along with Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, is celebrated as the site of Japan's first academically documented archaeological excavation. Under Tokugawa Mitsukuni's orders, the excavation aimed to explore and preserve the cultural heritage of the Nasu region. The discovery of burial goods, followed by their careful documentation and reburial, marks a significant milestone in Japanese archaeology and cultural preservation.<ref name="Asahi20230208"/>
The site was other_designation1 a National other_designation in 1951 as part of the "Samuraizuka Kofun" complex.<ref name="Nationalother_designation">{{Cite database|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/401/483|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs}}.</ref>
== Related Sites == * '''Shimosamuraizuka Kofun:''' Located nearby, this zenpō-kōhō-fun complements Kamisamuraizuka in historical and archaeological importance. * '''Kamisamuraizuka North Kofun:''' A smaller zenpō-kōhō-fun situated north of Kamisamuraizuka. Little is known about this kofun due to the lack of detailed investigations.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Further Reading == * [https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/115806 "Kamisamuraizuka Kofun Excavation Reports"] (Tochigi Future Creation Foundation, 2021). * [https://kotobank.jp/word/%E4%BE%8D%E5%A1%9A%E5%8F%A4%E5%A2%B3-69803 "Samuraizuka Kofun"] - Kotobank entry.

So a lot of these pages might not be marked on our wiki as having unknown parameters. But specifically keeping this comment here in case of import, these parameters should be added to [[Template:infobox ancient site]] rather than deleted from this draft or article[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:01, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=I hope this is not too annoying but this article is gonna be placed under [[:Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] and there is a decent chance these unknown parameters are ones[[Category:marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] I want added to the main template like burial chamber structure and burial goods. Please do not use any bots that just remove the unknown parameters without looking at them. This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun}} |name = Kamisamuraizuka Kofun |image = [[File:Kamisamuraizuka Kofun, funkyu-2.jpg|280px]] |caption = Mound (left: posterior section, right: anterior section) |alternate_name = Kurumazuka | part_of = |location = {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市}}, {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, Japan |coordinates = {{coord|36|48|21.05|N|140|7|29.45|E|region:JP-09_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |type = {{ill|Zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} |area = Mound length: 114m, height: 11.5m (posterior section) |burial = Clay chamber |burial goods = Bronze mirror, tubular beads, stone armlets, iron objects, haji pottery |Subordinate Tombs = |built = Late 4th century |Buried Person = |Mausoleum = |alternate_name = Included in the National other_designation "Samuraizuka Kofun" |Cultural Asset designation1 = |notes = Second largest zenpō-kōhō-fun in Tochigi Prefecture |map = Japan Tochigi |type = Zenpō-kōhō-fun-S |map_dot_label = Kamisamuraizuka Kofun |map_dot_label = top }}
'''Kamisamuraizuka Kofun''' (上侍塚古墳) is a {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} located in {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市}}, {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, Japan. Together with {{ill|Shimosamuraizuka Kofun|ja|下侍塚古墳}}, it is part of the National other_designation "Samuraizuka Kofun." This kofun is notable for being one of the first to undergo a scholarly excavation during the Edo period under the orders of {{ill|Tokugawa Mitsukuni|ja|徳川光圀}}, known as "Mito Kōmon."
== Overview == Kamisamuraizuka Kofun is a large zenpō-kōhō-fun constructed on the right bank of the {{ill|Naka River|ja|那珂川}}. Alongside Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, it represents one of the most significant ancient Kofuns in the Nasu region. Excavations date back to the 5th year of the Genroku era (1692), and further archaeological surveys were conducted starting in 2021.
The mound faces south, with a total length of 114 meters, making it the largest zenpō-kōhō-fun in the Nasu area and the second largest in Tochigi Prefecture after the {{ill|Fujimoto Kannonyama Kofun|ja|藤本観音山古墳}} in {{ill|Ashikaga|ja|足利市}}.<ref name="OhtawaraCity">[https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082767660/ Samuraizuka Kofun] (Ōtawara City website).</ref> The mound is covered with fukiishi (paving stones) and surrounded by a moat approximately 20 meters wide. The burial chamber is believed to have been constructed with clay.<ref name="descriptionboard">Site explanation board.</ref>
burial goods recovered during the Edo period include bronze mirrors, tubular beads, stone armlets, and iron objects such as armor fragments. These items were reburied in a wooden box following the excavation.<ref name="Asahi20230208">"Rediscovery Efforts for Edo built burial goods," ''Asahi Shimbun'' (February 8, 2023).</ref>
== area == The area of the mound are as follows:<ref name="OhtawaraCity"/><ref name="descriptionboard"/> * '''Mound length:''' 114 meters * '''Posterior section:''' ** Length: 60.5 meters ** Width: 58 meters ** Height: 11.5 meters * '''Anterior section:''' ** Length: 53.5 meters ** Width: 52 meters ** Height: 6.5 meters
The surrounding moat is visible on the north, west, and south sides, while the east side features a natural slope leading to the Naka River.
== Historical Significance == Kamisamuraizuka Kofun, along with Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, is celebrated as the site of Japan's first academically documented archaeological excavation. Under Tokugawa Mitsukuni's orders, the excavation aimed to explore and preserve the cultural heritage of the Nasu region. The discovery of burial goods, followed by their careful documentation and reburial, marks a significant milestone in Japanese archaeology and cultural preservation.<ref name="Asahi20230208"/>
The site was other_designation1 a National other_designation in 1951 as part of the "Samuraizuka Kofun" complex.<ref name="Nationalother_designation">{{Cite database|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/401/483|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs}}.</ref>
== Related Sites == * '''Shimosamuraizuka Kofun:''' Located nearby, this zenpō-kōhō-fun complements Kamisamuraizuka in historical and archaeological importance. * '''Kamisamuraizuka North Kofun:''' A smaller zenpō-kōhō-fun situated north of Kamisamuraizuka. Little is known about this kofun due to the lack of detailed investigations.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Further Reading == * [https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/115806 "Kamisamuraizuka Kofun Excavation Reports"] (Tochigi Future Creation Foundation, 2021). * [https://kotobank.jp/word/%E4%BE%8D%E5%A1%9A%E5%8F%A4%E5%A2%B3-69803 "Samuraizuka Kofun"] - Kotobank entry.
== External Links == * {{Cite database|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/401/483|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs}} * [https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082767660/ Samuraizuka Kofun] - Ōtawara City website.

burial goods recovered during the Edo period include bronze mirrors, tubular beads, stone armlets, and iron objects such as armor fragments. These items were reburied in a wooden box following the excavation.<ref name="Asahi20230208">"Rediscovery Efforts for Edo built burial goods," ''Asahi Shimbun'' (February 8, 2023).</ref>
== area == The area of the mound are as follows:<ref name="OhtawaraCity"/><ref name="descriptionboard"/> * '''Mound length:''' 114 meters * '''Posterior section:''' ** Length: 60.5 meters ** Width: 58 meters ** Height: 11.5 meters * '''Anterior section:''' ** Length: 53.5 meters ** Width: 52 meters ** Height: 6.5 meters
The surrounding moat is visible on the north, west, and south sides, while the east side features a natural slope leading to the Naka River.
== Historical Significance == Kamisamuraizuka Kofun, along with Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, is celebrated as the site of Japan's first academically documented archaeological excavation. Under Tokugawa Mitsukuni's orders, the excavation aimed to explore and preserve the cultural heritage of the Nasu region. The discovery of burial goods, followed by their careful documentation and reburial, marks a significant milestone in Japanese archaeology and cultural preservation.<ref name="Asahi20230208"/>
The site was Historic Site1 a National Historic Site in 1951 as part of the "Samuraizuka Kofun" complex.<ref name="NationalHistoric Site">{{Cite database|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/401/483|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs}}.</ref>
== Related Sites == * '''Shimosamuraizuka Kofun:''' Located nearby, this zenpō-kōhō-fun complements Kamisamuraizuka in historical and archaeological importance. * '''Kamisamuraizuka North Kofun:''' A smaller zenpō-kōhō-fun situated north of Kamisamuraizuka. Little is known about this kofun due to the lack of detailed investigations.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Further Reading == * [https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/115806 "Kamisamuraizuka Kofun Excavation Reports"] (Tochigi Future Creation Foundation, 2021). * [https://kotobank.jp/word/%E4%BE%8D%E5%A1%9A%E5%8F%A4%E5%A2%B3-69803 "Samuraizuka Kofun"] - Kotobank entry.
== External Links == * {{Cite database|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/401/483|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs}} * [https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082767660/ Samuraizuka Kofun] - Ōtawara City website.

'''Kamisamuraizuka Kofun''' (上侍塚古墳) is a {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} located in {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市}}, {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, Japan. Together with {{ill|Shimosamuraizuka Kofun|ja|下侍塚古墳}}, it is part of the National Historic Site "Samuraizuka Kofun." This kofun is notable for being one of the first to undergo a scholarly excavation during the Edo period under the orders of {{ill|Tokugawa Mitsukuni|ja|徳川光圀}}, known as "Mito Kōmon."
== Overview == Kamisamuraizuka Kofun is a large zenpō-kōhō-fun constructed on the right bank of the {{ill|Naka River|ja|那珂川}}. Alongside Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, it represents one of the most significant ancient Kofuns in the Nasu region. Excavations date back to the 5th year of the Genroku era (1692), and further archaeological surveys were conducted starting in 2021.
The mound faces south, with a total length of 114 meters, making it the largest zenpō-kōhō-fun in the Nasu area and the second largest in Tochigi Prefecture after the {{ill|Fujimoto Kannonyama Kofun|ja|藤本観音山古墳}} in {{ill|Ashikaga|ja|足利市}}.<ref name="OhtawaraCity">[https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082767660/ Samuraizuka Kofun] (Ōtawara City website).</ref> The mound is covered with fukiishi (paving stones) and surrounded by a moat approximately 20 meters wide. The burial chamber is believed to have been constructed with clay.<ref name="descriptionboard">Site explanation board.</ref>
burial goods recovered during the Edo period include bronze mirrors, tubular beads, stone armlets, and iron objects such as armor fragments. These items were reburied in a wooden box following the excavation.<ref name="Asahi20230208">"Rediscovery Efforts for Edo built burial goods," ''Asahi Shimbun'' (February 8, 2023).</ref>
== area == The area of the mound are as follows:<ref name="OhtawaraCity"/><ref name="descriptionboard"/> * '''Mound length:''' 114 meters * '''Posterior section:''' ** Length: 60.5 meters ** Width: 58 meters ** Height: 11.5 meters * '''Anterior section:''' ** Length: 53.5 meters ** Width: 52 meters ** Height: 6.5 meters
The surrounding moat is visible on the north, west, and south sides, while the east side features a natural slope leading to the Naka River.
== Historical Significance == Kamisamuraizuka Kofun, along with Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, is celebrated as the site of Japan's first academically documented archaeological excavation. Under Tokugawa Mitsukuni's orders, the excavation aimed to explore and preserve the cultural heritage of the Nasu region. The discovery of burial goods, followed by their careful documentation and reburial, marks a significant milestone in Japanese archaeology and cultural preservation.<ref name="Asahi20230208"/>
The site was Historic Site1 a National Historic Site in 1951 as part of the "Samuraizuka Kofun" complex.<ref name="NationalHistoric Site">{{Cite database|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/401/483|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs}}.</ref>
== Related Sites == * '''Shimosamuraizuka Kofun:''' Located nearby, this zenpō-kōhō-fun complements Kamisamuraizuka in historical and archaeological importance. * '''Kamisamuraizuka North Kofun:''' A smaller zenpō-kōhō-fun situated north of Kamisamuraizuka. Little is known about this kofun due to the lack of detailed investigations.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Further Reading == * [https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/115806 "Kamisamuraizuka Kofun Excavation Reports"] (Tochigi Future Creation Foundation, 2021). * [https://kotobank.jp/word/%E4%BE%8D%E5%A1%9A%E5%8F%A4%E5%A2%B3-69803 "Samuraizuka Kofun"] - Kotobank entry.

The site was Historic Site1 a National Historic Site in 1951 as part of the "Samuraizuka Kofun" complex.<ref name="NationalHistoric Site">{{Cite database|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/401/483|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs}}.</ref>
== Related Sites == * '''Shimosamuraizuka Kofun:''' Located nearby, this zenpō-kōhō-fun complements Kamisamuraizuka in historical and archaeological importance. * '''Kamisamuraizuka North Kofun:''' A smaller zenpō-kōhō-fun situated north of Kamisamuraizuka. Little is known about this kofun due to the lack of detailed investigations.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Further Reading == * [https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/115806 "Kamisamuraizuka Kofun Excavation Reports"] (Tochigi Future Creation Foundation, 2021). * [https://kotobank.jp/word/%E4%BE%8D%E5%A1%9A%E5%8F%A4%E5%A2%B3-69803 "Samuraizuka Kofun"] - Kotobank entry.

So a lot of these pages might not be marked on our wiki as having unknown parameters. But specifically keeping this comment here in case of import, these parameters should be added to [[Template:infobox ancient site]] rather than deleted from this draft or article[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:01, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=I hope this is not too annoying but this article is gonna be placed under [[:Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] and there is a decent chance these unknown parameters are ones I want added to the main template like burial chamber structure and burial goods. Please do not use any bots that just remove the unknown parameters without looking at them. This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|12=draft}} |name = Kamisamuraizuka Kofun |image = [[File:Kamisamuraizuka Kofun, funkyu-2.jpg|280px]] |caption = Mound (left: posterior section, right: anterior section) |alternate_name = Kurumazuka | part_of = |location = {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県|12=draft}}, Japan |coordinates = {{coord|36|48|21.05|N|140|7|29.45|E|region:JP-09_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |type = {{ill|Zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun|12=draft}} |area = Mound length: 114m, height: 11.5m (posterior section) |burial = Clay chamber |burial goods = Bronze mirror, tubular beads, stone armlets, iron objects, haji pottery |Subordinate Tombs = |built = Late 4th century |Buried Person = |Mausoleum = |alternate_name = Included in the National Historic Site "Samuraizuka Kofun" |Cultural Asset designation1 = |notes = Second largest zenpō-kōhō-fun in Tochigi Prefecture |map = Japan Tochigi |type = Zenpō-kōhō-fun-S |map_dot_label = Kamisamuraizuka Kofun |map_dot_label = top }}
'''Kamisamuraizuka Kofun''' (上侍塚古墳) is a {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun|12=draft}} located in {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県|12=draft}}, Japan. Together with {{ill|Shimosamuraizuka Kofun|ja|下侍塚古墳|12=draft}}, it is part of the National Historic Site "Samuraizuka Kofun." This kofun is notable for being one of the first to undergo a scholarly excavation during the Edo period under the orders of {{ill|Tokugawa Mitsukuni|ja|徳川光圀|12=draft}}, known as "Mito Kōmon."
== Overview == Kamisamuraizuka Kofun is a large zenpō-kōhō-fun constructed on the right bank of the {{ill|Naka River|ja|那珂川|12=draft}}. Alongside Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, it represents one of the most significant ancient Kofuns in the Nasu region. Excavations date back to the 5th year of the Genroku era (1692), and further archaeological surveys were conducted starting in 2021.
The mound faces south, with a total length of 114 meters, making it the largest zenpō-kōhō-fun in the Nasu area and the second largest in Tochigi Prefecture after the {{ill|Fujimoto Kannonyama Kofun|ja|藤本観音山古墳|12=draft}} in {{ill|Ashikaga|ja|足利市|12=draft}}.<ref name="OhtawaraCity">[https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082767660/ Samuraizuka Kofun] (Ōtawara City website).</ref> The mound is covered with fukiishi (paving stones) and surrounded by a moat approximately 20 meters wide. The burial chamber is believed to have been constructed with clay.<ref name="descriptionboard">Site explanation board.</ref>
burial goods recovered during the Edo period include bronze mirrors, tubular beads, stone armlets, and iron objects such as armor fragments. These items were reburied in a wooden box following the excavation.<ref name="Asahi20230208">"Rediscovery Efforts for Edo built burial goods," ''Asahi Shimbun'' (February 8, 2023).</ref>
== area == The area of the mound are as follows:<ref name="OhtawaraCity"/><ref name="descriptionboard"/> * '''Mound length:''' 114 meters * '''Posterior section:''' ** Length: 60.5 meters ** Width: 58 meters ** Height: 11.5 meters * '''Anterior section:''' ** Length: 53.5 meters ** Width: 52 meters ** Height: 6.5 meters
The surrounding moat is visible on the north, west, and south sides, while the east side features a natural slope leading to the Naka River.
== Historical Significance == Kamisamuraizuka Kofun, along with Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, is celebrated as the site of Japan's first academically documented archaeological excavation. Under Tokugawa Mitsukuni's orders, the excavation aimed to explore and preserve the cultural heritage of the Nasu region. The discovery of burial goods, followed by their careful documentation and reburial, marks a significant milestone in Japanese archaeology and cultural preservation.<ref name="Asahi20230208"/>
The site was Historic Site1 a National Historic Site in 1951 as part of the "Samuraizuka Kofun" complex.<ref name="NationalHistoric Site">{{Cite database|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/401/483|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs}}.</ref>
== Related Sites == * '''Shimosamuraizuka Kofun:''' Located nearby, this zenpō-kōhō-fun complements Kamisamuraizuka in historical and archaeological importance. * '''Kamisamuraizuka North Kofun:''' A smaller zenpō-kōhō-fun situated north of Kamisamuraizuka. Little is known about this kofun due to the lack of detailed investigations.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Further Reading == * [https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/115806 "Kamisamuraizuka Kofun Excavation Reports"] (Tochigi Future Creation Foundation, 2021). * [https://kotobank.jp/word/%E4%BE%8D%E5%A1%9A%E5%8F%A4%E5%A2%B3-69803 "Samuraizuka Kofun"] - Kotobank entry.
== External Links == * {{Cite database|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/401/483|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs}} * [https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082767660/ Samuraizuka Kofun] - Ōtawara City website.

'''Kamisamuraizuka Kofun''' (上侍塚古墳) is a {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun|12=draft|12=draft}} located in {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県|12=draft|12=draft}}, Japan. Together with {{ill|Shimosamuraizuka Kofun|ja|下侍塚古墳|12=draft|12=draft}}, it is part of the National Historic Site "Samuraizuka Kofun." This kofun is notable for being one of the first to undergo a scholarly excavation during the Edo period under the orders of {{ill|Tokugawa Mitsukuni|ja|徳川光圀|12=draft|12=draft}}, known as "Mito Kōmon."
== Overview == Kamisamuraizuka Kofun is a large zenpō-kōhō-fun constructed on the right bank of the {{ill|Naka River|ja|那珂川|12=draft|12=draft}}. Alongside Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, it represents one of the most significant ancient Kofuns in the Nasu region. Excavations date back to the 5th year of the Genroku era (1692), and further archaeological surveys were conducted starting in 2021.
The mound faces south, with a total length of 114 meters, making it the largest zenpō-kōhō-fun in the Nasu area and the second largest in Tochigi Prefecture after the {{ill|Fujimoto Kannonyama Kofun|ja|藤本観音山古墳|12=draft|12=draft}} in {{ill|Ashikaga|ja|足利市|12=draft|12=draft}}.<ref name="OhtawaraCity">[https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082767660/ Samuraizuka Kofun] (Ōtawara City website).</ref> The mound is covered with fukiishi (paving stones) and surrounded by a moat approximately 20 meters wide. The burial chamber is believed to have been constructed with clay.<ref name="descriptionboard">Site explanation board.</ref>
burial goods recovered during the Edo period include bronze mirrors, tubular beads, stone armlets, and iron objects such as armor fragments. These items were reburied in a wooden box following the excavation.<ref name="Asahi20230208">"Rediscovery Efforts for Edo built burial goods," ''Asahi Shimbun'' (February 8, 2023).</ref>
== area == The area of the mound are as follows:<ref name="OhtawaraCity"/><ref name="descriptionboard"/> * '''Mound length:''' 114 meters * '''Posterior section:''' ** Length: 60.5 meters ** Width: 58 meters ** Height: 11.5 meters * '''Anterior section:''' ** Length: 53.5 meters ** Width: 52 meters ** Height: 6.5 meters
The surrounding moat is visible on the north, west, and south sides, while the east side features a natural slope leading to the Naka River.
== Historical Significance == Kamisamuraizuka Kofun, along with Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, is celebrated as the site of Japan's first academically documented archaeological excavation. Under Tokugawa Mitsukuni's orders, the excavation aimed to explore and preserve the cultural heritage of the Nasu region. The discovery of burial goods, followed by their careful documentation and reburial, marks a significant milestone in Japanese archaeology and cultural preservation.<ref name="Asahi20230208"/>
The site was Historic Site1 a National Historic Site in 1951 as part of the "Samuraizuka Kofun" complex.<ref name="NationalHistoric Site">{{Cite database|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/401/483|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs}}.</ref>
== Related Sites == * '''Shimosamuraizuka Kofun:''' Located nearby, this zenpō-kōhō-fun complements Kamisamuraizuka in historical and archaeological importance. * '''Kamisamuraizuka North Kofun:''' A smaller zenpō-kōhō-fun situated north of Kamisamuraizuka. Little is known about this kofun due to the lack of detailed investigations.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Further Reading == * [https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/115806 "Kamisamuraizuka Kofun Excavation Reports"] (Tochigi Future Creation Foundation, 2021). * [https://kotobank.jp/word/%E4%BE%8D%E5%A1%9A%E5%8F%A4%E5%A2%B3-69803 "Samuraizuka Kofun"] - Kotobank entry.
== External Links == * {{Cite database|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/401/483|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs}} * [https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082767660/ Samuraizuka Kofun] - Ōtawara City website.

This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|12=draft|12=draft}} |name = Kamisamuraizuka Kofun |image = [[File:Kamisamuraizuka Kofun, funkyu-2.jpg|280px]] |caption = Mound (left: posterior section, right: anterior section) |alternate_name = Kurumazuka | part_of = |location = {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県|12=draft|12=draft}}, Japan |coordinates = {{coord|36|48|21.05|N|140|7|29.45|E|region:JP-09_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |type = {{ill|Zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun|12=draft|12=draft}} |area = Mound length: 114m, height: 11.5m (posterior section) |burial = Clay chamber |burial goods = Bronze mirror, tubular beads, stone armlets, iron objects, haji pottery |Subordinate Tombs = |built = Late 4th century |Buried Person = |Mausoleum = |alternate_name = Included in the National Historic Site "Samuraizuka Kofun" |Cultural Asset designation1 = |notes = Second largest zenpō-kōhō-fun in Tochigi Prefecture |map = Japan Tochigi |type = Zenpō-kōhō-fun-S |map_dot_label = Kamisamuraizuka Kofun |map_dot_label = top }}
'''Kamisamuraizuka Kofun''' (上侍塚古墳) is a {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun|12=draft|12=draft}} located in {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県|12=draft|12=draft}}, Japan. Together with {{ill|Shimosamuraizuka Kofun|ja|下侍塚古墳|12=draft|12=draft}}, it is part of the National Historic Site "Samuraizuka Kofun." This kofun is notable for being one of the first to undergo a scholarly excavation during the Edo period under the orders of {{ill|Tokugawa Mitsukuni|ja|徳川光圀|12=draft|12=draft}}, known as "Mito Kōmon."
== Overview == Kamisamuraizuka Kofun is a large zenpō-kōhō-fun constructed on the right bank of the {{ill|Naka River|ja|那珂川|12=draft|12=draft}}. Alongside Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, it represents one of the most significant ancient Kofuns in the Nasu region. Excavations date back to the 5th year of the Genroku era (1692), and further archaeological surveys were conducted starting in 2021.
The mound faces south, with a total length of 114 meters, making it the largest zenpō-kōhō-fun in the Nasu area and the second largest in Tochigi Prefecture after the {{ill|Fujimoto Kannonyama Kofun|ja|藤本観音山古墳|12=draft|12=draft}} in {{ill|Ashikaga|ja|足利市|12=draft|12=draft}}.<ref name="OhtawaraCity">[https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082767660/ Samuraizuka Kofun] (Ōtawara City website).</ref> The mound is covered with fukiishi (paving stones) and surrounded by a moat approximately 20 meters wide. The burial chamber is believed to have been constructed with clay.<ref name="descriptionboard">Site explanation board.</ref>
burial goods recovered during the Edo period include bronze mirrors, tubular beads, stone armlets, and iron objects such as armor fragments. These items were reburied in a wooden box following the excavation.<ref name="Asahi20230208">"Rediscovery Efforts for Edo built burial goods," ''Asahi Shimbun'' (February 8, 2023).</ref>
== area == The area of the mound are as follows:<ref name="OhtawaraCity"/><ref name="descriptionboard"/> * '''Mound length:''' 114 meters * '''Posterior section:''' ** Length: 60.5 meters ** Width: 58 meters ** Height: 11.5 meters * '''Anterior section:''' ** Length: 53.5 meters ** Width: 52 meters ** Height: 6.5 meters
The surrounding moat is visible on the north, west, and south sides, while the east side features a natural slope leading to the Naka River.
== Historical Significance == Kamisamuraizuka Kofun, along with Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, is celebrated as the site of Japan's first academically documented archaeological excavation. Under Tokugawa Mitsukuni's orders, the excavation aimed to explore and preserve the cultural heritage of the Nasu region. The discovery of burial goods, followed by their careful documentation and reburial, marks a significant milestone in Japanese archaeology and cultural preservation.<ref name="Asahi20230208"/>
The site was Historic Site1 a National Historic Site in 1951 as part of the "Samuraizuka Kofun" complex.<ref name="NationalHistoric Site">{{Cite database|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/401/483|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs}}.</ref>
== Related Sites == * '''Shimosamuraizuka Kofun:''' Located nearby, this zenpō-kōhō-fun complements Kamisamuraizuka in historical and archaeological importance. * '''Kamisamuraizuka North Kofun:''' A smaller zenpō-kōhō-fun situated north of Kamisamuraizuka. Little is known about this kofun due to the lack of detailed investigations.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Further Reading == * [https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/115806 "Kamisamuraizuka Kofun Excavation Reports"] (Tochigi Future Creation Foundation, 2021). * [https://kotobank.jp/word/%E4%BE%8D%E5%A1%9A%E5%8F%A4%E5%A2%B3-69803 "Samuraizuka Kofun"] - Kotobank entry.
== External Links == * {{Cite database|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/401/483|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs}} * [https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082767660/ Samuraizuka Kofun] - Ōtawara City website.

'''Kamisamuraizuka Kofun''' (上侍塚古墳) is a {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun|12=draft|12=draft}} located in {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県|12=draft|12=draft}}, Japan. Together with {{ill|Shimosamuraizuka Kofun|ja|下侍塚古墳|12=draft|12=draft}}, it is part of the National Historic Site "Samuraizuka Kofun." This kofun is notable for being one of the first to undergo a scholarly excavation during the Edo period under the orders of {{ill|Tokugawa Mitsukuni|ja|徳川光圀|12=draft|12=draft}}, known as "Mito Kōmon."
== Overview == Kamisamuraizuka Kofun is a large zenpō-kōhō-fun constructed on the right bank of the {{ill|Naka River|ja|那珂川|12=draft|12=draft}}. Alongside Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, it represents one of the most significant ancient Kofuns in the Nasu region. Excavations date back to the 5th year of the Genroku era (1692), and further archaeological surveys were conducted starting in 2021.
The mound faces south, with a total length of 114 meters, making it the largest zenpō-kōhō-fun in the Nasu area and the second largest in Tochigi Prefecture after the {{ill|Fujimoto Kannonyama Kofun|ja|藤本観音山古墳|12=draft|12=draft}} in {{ill|Ashikaga|ja|足利市|12=draft|12=draft}}.<ref name="OhtawaraCity">[https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082767660/ Samuraizuka Kofun] (Ōtawara City website).</ref> The mound is covered with fukiishi (paving stones) and surrounded by a moat approximately 20 meters wide. The burial chamber is believed to have been constructed with clay.<ref name="descriptionboard">Site explanation board.</ref>
burial goods recovered during the Edo period include bronze mirrors, tubular beads, stone armlets, and iron objects such as armor fragments. These items were reburied in a wooden box following the excavation.<ref name="Asahi20230208">"Rediscovery Efforts for Edo built burial goods," ''Asahi Shimbun'' (February 8, 2023).</ref>
== area == The area of the mound are as follows:<ref name="OhtawaraCity"/><ref name="descriptionboard"/> * '''Mound length:''' 114 meters * '''Posterior section:''' ** Length: 60.5 meters ** Width: 58 meters ** Height: 11.5 meters * '''Anterior section:''' ** Length: 53.5 meters ** Width: 52 meters ** Height: 6.5 meters
The surrounding moat is visible on the north, west, and south sides, while the east side features a natural slope leading to the Naka River.
== Historical Significance == Kamisamuraizuka Kofun, along with Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, is celebrated as the site of Japan's first academically documented archaeological excavation. Under Tokugawa Mitsukuni's orders, the excavation aimed to explore and preserve the cultural heritage of the Nasu region. The discovery of burial goods, followed by their careful documentation and reburial, marks a significant milestone in Japanese archaeology and cultural preservation.<ref name="Asahi20230208"/>
The site was Historic Site1 a National Historic Site in 1951 as part of the "Samuraizuka Kofun" complex.<ref name="NationalHistoric Site">{{Cite database|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/401/483|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs}}.</ref>
== Related Sites == * '''Shimosamuraizuka Kofun:''' Located nearby, this zenpō-kōhō-fun complements Kamisamuraizuka in historical and archaeological importance. * '''Kamisamuraizuka North Kofun:''' A smaller zenpō-kōhō-fun situated north of Kamisamuraizuka. Little is known about this kofun due to the lack of detailed investigations.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Further Reading == * [https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/115806 "Kamisamuraizuka Kofun Excavation Reports"] (Tochigi Future Creation Foundation, 2021). * [https://kotobank.jp/word/%E4%BE%8D%E5%A1%9A%E5%8F%A4%E5%A2%B3-69803 "Samuraizuka Kofun"] - Kotobank entry.
== External Links == * {{Cite database|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/401/483|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs}} * [https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082767660/ Samuraizuka Kofun] - Ōtawara City website.

'''Kamisamuraizuka Kofun''' (上侍塚古墳) is a {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun|12=draft|12=draft}} located in {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県|12=draft|12=draft}}, Japan. Together with {{ill|Shimosamuraizuka Kofun|ja|下侍塚古墳|12=draft|12=draft}}, it is part of the National Historic Site "Samuraizuka Kofun." This kofun is notable for being one of the first to undergo a scholarly excavation during the Edo period under the orders of {{ill|Tokugawa Mitsukuni|ja|徳川光圀|12=draft|12=draft}}, known as "Mito Kōmon."
== Overview == Kamisamuraizuka Kofun is a large zenpō-kōhō-fun constructed on the right bank of the {{ill|Naka River|ja|那珂川|12=draft|12=draft}}. Alongside Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, it represents one of the most significant ancient Kofuns in the Nasu region. Excavations date back to the 5th year of the Genroku era (1692), and further archaeological surveys were conducted starting in 2021.
The mound faces south, with a total length of 114 meters, making it the largest zenpō-kōhō-fun in the Nasu area and the second largest in Tochigi Prefecture after the {{ill|Fujimoto Kannonyama Kofun|ja|藤本観音山古墳|12=draft|12=draft}} in {{ill|Ashikaga|ja|足利市|12=draft|12=draft}}.<ref name="OhtawaraCity">[https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082767660/ Samuraizuka Kofun] (Ōtawara City website).</ref> The mound is covered with fukiishi (paving stones) and surrounded by a moat approximately 20 meters wide. The burial chamber is believed to have been constructed with clay.<ref name="descriptionboard">Site explanation board.</ref>
burial goods recovered during the Edo period include bronze mirrors, tubular beads, stone armlets, and iron objects such as armor fragments. These items were reburied in a wooden box following the excavation.<ref name="Asahi20230208">"Rediscovery Efforts for Edo built burial goods," ''Asahi Shimbun'' (February 8, 2023).</ref>
== area == The area of the mound are as follows:<ref name="OhtawaraCity"/><ref name="descriptionboard"/> * '''Mound length:''' 114 meters * '''Posterior section:''' ** Length: 60.5 meters ** Width: 58 meters ** Height: 11.5 meters * '''Anterior section:''' ** Length: 53.5 meters ** Width: 52 meters ** Height: 6.5 meters
The surrounding moat is visible on the north, west, and south sides, while the east side features a natural slope leading to the Naka River.
== Historical Significance == Kamisamuraizuka Kofun, along with Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, is celebrated as the site of Japan's first academically documented archaeological excavation. Under Tokugawa Mitsukuni's orders, the excavation aimed to explore and preserve the cultural heritage of the Nasu region. The discovery of burial goods, followed by their careful documentation and reburial, marks a significant milestone in Japanese archaeology and cultural preservation.<ref name="Asahi20230208"/>
The site was Historic Site1 a National Historic Site in 1951 as part of the "Samuraizuka Kofun" complex.<ref name="NationalHistoric Site">{{Cite database|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/401/483|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs}}.</ref>
== Related Sites == * '''Shimosamuraizuka Kofun:''' Located nearby, this zenpō-kōhō-fun complements Kamisamuraizuka in historical and archaeological importance. * '''Kamisamuraizuka North Kofun:''' A smaller zenpō-kōhō-fun situated north of Kamisamuraizuka. Little is known about this kofun due to the lack of detailed investigations.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Further Reading == * [https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/115806 "Kamisamuraizuka Kofun Excavation Reports"] (Tochigi Future Creation Foundation, 2021). * [https://kotobank.jp/word/%E4%BE%8D%E5%A1%9A%E5%8F%A4%E5%A2%B3-69803 "Samuraizuka Kofun"] - Kotobank entry.
== External Links == * {{Cite database|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/401/483|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs}} * [https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082767660/ Samuraizuka Kofun] - Ōtawara City website.
